abstract: The rapid advancement of Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) for 2D images and videos has motivated extending these models to understand 3D scenes, aiming for human-like visual-spatial intelligence. Nevertheless, achieving deep spatial understanding comparable to human capabilities poses significant challenges in model encoding and data acquisition. Existing methods frequently depend on external depth sensors for geometry capture or utilize off-the-shelf algorithms for pre-constructing 3D maps, thereby limiting their scalability, especially with prevalent monocular video inputs and for time-sensitive applications. In this work, we introduce VLM-3R, a unified framework for Vision-Language Models (VLMs) that incorporates 3D Reconstructive instruction tuning. VLM-3R processes monocular video frames by employing a geometry encoder to derive implicit 3D tokens that represent spatial understanding. Leveraging our Spatial-Visual-View Fusion and over 200K curated 3D reconstructive instruction tuning question-answer (QA) pairs, VLM-3R effectively aligns real-world spatial context with language instructions. This enables monocular 3D spatial assistance and embodied reasoning. To facilitate the evaluation of temporal reasoning, we introduce the Vision-Spatial-Temporal Intelligence benchmark, featuring over 138.6K QA pairs across five distinct tasks focused on evolving spatial relationships. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our model, VLM-3R, not only facilitates robust visual-spatial reasoning but also enables the understanding of temporal 3D context changes, excelling in both accuracy and scalability.

keyInsights: Integrating implicit 3D reconstructive tokens (spatial and view tokens) from a metric-scale geometry encoder allows VLMs to achieve robust 3D spatial and temporal reasoning directly from monocular video without explicit depth sensors.

Explicitly encoding camera pose and motion information (via view tokens) enables VLMs to effectively disentangle camera dynamics from object-centric spatial relationships, crucial for temporal 3D understanding.

Automated generation of diverse 3D-aware instruction-tuning data and a dedicated spatio-temporal benchmark (VSTI-Bench) are essential for training and evaluating VLMs with advanced spatial and temporal intelligence.
originalProblem: Existing Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) struggle with human-like visual-spatial intelligence in 3D scenes from monocular video, often relying on specialized depth sensors or slow, multi-stage 3D reconstruction pipelines.
Traditional 3D reconstruction methods frequently yield scale-ambiguous geometric representations, hindering accurate metric spatial estimations in VLMs.
Current LMMs lack robust temporal reasoning about dynamic changes in spatial relationships caused by camera motion or evolving object layouts over time.
results: VLM-3R (7B) achieved an average score of 60.9% on VSI-Bench, outperforming finetuned LLaVA-NeXT-Video (57.7%) and showing significant gains in metric-aware tasks (e.g., Absolute Distance improved from 20.2% to 49.4%).
On the VSTI-Bench for spatio-temporal reasoning, VLM-3R (7B) led all models with 58.8%, surpassing proprietary models like Gemini-2.5 Pro (42.4%) and GPT-4o (38.2%).

The framework generalized well to embodied exploration on OST-Bench with 42.9%, outperforming its base model (LLaVA-Video-7B at 39.3%), and retained strong general visual understanding on Video-MME (59.9%) and VQA v2 (52.57%).
solution: Introduces VLM-3R, an end-to-end framework using a pre-trained CUT3R geometry encoder to extract metric-scale 3D point maps and relative camera poses as "3D reconstructive tokens" (spatial and view tokens) from monocular video.
Employs a Spatial-Visual-View Fusion module to integrate these 3D tokens with native 2D visual tokens via cross-attention, enriching visual representations for the LMM backbone.

Developed a scalable pipeline to generate over 200K diverse 3D reconstructive instruction-tuning QA pairs and 4,225 embodied route-planning instances, along with a new VSTI-Bench for spatio-temporal reasoning evaluation.
paper_summary.summary: VLM-3R is a framework that augments Vision-Language Models with 3D spatial and temporal reasoning from monocular video. It introduces implicit 3D reconstructive tokens and a fusion mechanism, achieving leading performance on new spatial and spatio-temporal benchmarks, including outperforming proprietary models on VSTI-Bench.

title: VLM-3R: Vision-Language Models Augmented with Instruction-Aligned 3D Reconstruction

abstract:
Recent advancements in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have
significantly enhanced performance on 2D visual tasks. However, improving their
spatial intelligence remains a challenge. Existing 3D MLLMs always rely on
additional 3D or 2.5D data to incorporate spatial awareness, restricting their
utility in scenarios with only 2D inputs, such as images or videos. In this
paper, we present Spatial-MLLM, a novel framework for visual-based spatial
reasoning from purely 2D observations. Unlike conventional video MLLMs which
rely on CLIP-based visual encoders optimized for semantic understanding, our
key insight is to unleash the strong structure prior from the feed-forward
visual geometry foundation model. Specifically, we propose a dual-encoder
architecture: a pretrained 2D visual encoder to extract semantic features, and
a spatial encoder-initialized from the backbone of the visual geometry model-to
extract 3D structure features. A connector then integrates both features into
unified visual tokens for enhanced spatial understanding. Furthermore, we
propose a space-aware frame sampling strategy at inference time, which selects
the spatially informative frames of a video sequence, ensuring that even under
limited token length, the model focuses on frames critical for spatial
reasoning. Beyond architecture improvements, we construct the Spatial-MLLM-120k
dataset and train the model on it using supervised fine-tuning and GRPO.
Extensive experiments on various real-world datasets demonstrate that our
spatial-MLLM achieves state-of-the-art performance in a wide range of
visual-based spatial understanding and reasoning tasks. Project page:
this https URL

keyInsights: Visual geometry foundation models pretrained on pixel-point pairs can effectively recover implicit 3D structural information from 2D video inputs when properly integrated with semantic encoders
Frame sampling based on spatial informativeness rather than temporal uniformity significantly improves performance by focusing computational resources on geometrically relevant content
Reinforcement learning with cold-start initialization and task-dependent reward modeling enhances long-chain spatial reasoning capabilities beyond supervised fine-tuning alone
originalProblem: Existing video MLLMs rely on CLIP-based visual encoders trained on image-text pairs, which capture semantic content but lack spatial awareness needed for 3D reasoning
Current 3D MLLMs require additional 3D or 2.5D data (point clouds, depth maps) that limit their applicability in scenarios with only 2D video available
MLLMs struggle with visual-based spatial intelligence tasks including object counting, distance estimation, and spatial relationship understanding from monocular video
results: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on VSI-Bench, surpassing both proprietary and open-source MLLMs including models with significantly larger parameter counts
Substantially outperforms video-input models on ScanQA and SQA3D benchmarks, even exceeding some task-specific models and approaches using 3D/2.5D input data
Ablation studies confirm effectiveness of dual-encoder architecture, space-aware frame sampling, and GRPO training components for spatial reasoning enhancement
solution: Dual-encoder architecture combining a 2D visual encoder for semantic features with a spatial encoder initialized from VGGT visual geometry foundation model for 3D structural information
Space-aware frame sampling strategy that reformulates frame selection as a maximum coverage problem over voxel grids to identify spatially informative frames under input length constraints

Two-stage training pipeline with supervised fine-tuning on Spatial-MLLM-120k dataset followed by Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) reinforcement learning for enhanced chain-of-thought spatial reasoning
paper_summary.summary: Tsinghua University researchers develop Spatial-MLLM, a framework that enhances multimodal large language models' spatial reasoning from 2D videos by combining a standard visual encoder with a spatial encoder initialized from a visual geometry foundation model (VGGT), achieving state-of-the-art performance on VSI-Bench and substantially outperforming existing video MLLMs on ScanQA and SQA3D through a dual-encoder architecture, space-aware frame sampling strategy, and reinforcement learning training on their new Spatial-MLLM-120k dataset covering diverse spatial understanding tasks.

title: Spatial-MLLM: Boosting MLLM Capabilities in Visual-based Spatial  Intelligence

abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) still lack robustness in spatial intelligence, demonstrating poor performance on spatial understanding and reasoning tasks. We attribute this gap to the absence of a visual geometry learning process capable of reconstructing 3D space from 2D images. We present G$^2$VLM, a geometry grounded vision-language model that bridges two fundamental aspects of spatial intelligence: spatial 3D reconstruction and spatial understanding. G$^2$VLM natively leverages learned 3D visual geometry features to directly predict 3D attributes and enhance spatial reasoning tasks via in-context learning and interleaved reasoning. Our unified design is highly scalable for spatial understanding: it trains on abundant multi-view image and video data, while simultaneously leveraging the benefits of 3D visual priors that are typically only derived from hard-to-collect annotations. Experimental results demonstrate G$^2$VLM is proficient in both tasks, achieving comparable results to state-of-the-art feed-forward 3D reconstruction models and achieving better or competitive results across spatial understanding and reasoning tasks. By unifying a semantically strong VLM with low-level 3D vision tasks, we hope G$^2$VLM can serve as a strong baseline for the community and unlock more future applications, such as 3D scene editing.

keyInsights: Unifying low-level 3D reconstruction and high-level spatial understanding within a single VLM architecture significantly enhances the model's spatial intelligence.
A dual-expert design with shared attention allows for a rich interplay between geometric and semantic representations, enabling mutual improvement.

Explicitly learning 3D visual geometry from abundant 2D multi-view image and video data offers a scalable method to imbue VLMs with a genuine understanding of the 3D world, bypassing extensive 3D annotation needs.
originalProblem: Current Vision-Language Models (VLMs) often lack robust spatial intelligence, struggling with tasks requiring a deep understanding of 3D space.
VLMs traditionally learn implicit knowledge of the physical world from 2D image-text datasets, without explicit learning of visual geometry to lift 2D perceptions into coherent 3D representations.
Existing feedforward visual geometry models focus solely on 3D reconstruction, neglecting higher-level scene understanding and reasoning capabilities.
results: Achieves competitive visual geometry performance, reducing the Absolute Relative Error (Abs Rel) for monocular depth estimation on Sintel from VGGT’s 0.335 to 0.297.

Demonstrates state-of-the-art spatial reasoning, with G
G

²VLM-SR surpassing GPT-4o by 18.48 points on the SPAR-Bench and achieving the best results on MindCube and OmniSpatial benchmarks.

Ablation studies confirm the positive impact of explicit geometry pretraining and the dual-encoder design, showing that better geometric features directly correlate with improved spatial reasoning performance.
solution:
Introduces G
G

²VLM, a unified framework with a Mixture-of-Transformer-Experts (MoT) architecture, comprising a Geometric Perception Expert and a Semantic Perception Expert.

Employs a two-stage training strategy: initial pretraining of the geometric expert on 3D-annotated datasets, followed by joint training of both experts on spatial understanding and general VQA datasets.
The geometric expert predicts 3D attributes like depth, point clouds, and camera poses, with all tokens from both experts engaging in shared multi-modal self-attention.
paper_summary.summary:
G
G

²VLM integrates 3D reconstruction and spatial reasoning within a single Vision-Language Model, addressing the spatial intelligence limitations of current VLMs. It learns explicit visual geometry from 2D data using a Mixture-of-Transformer-Experts architecture, leading to robust spatial understanding and strong performance on both 3D reconstruction and complex spatial reasoning benchmarks.

title: G$^2$VLM: Geometry Grounded Vision Language Model with Unified 3D Reconstruction and Spatial Reasoning

abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) excel at 2D visual understanding but remain limited in their ability to reason about 3D space. In this work, we leverage large-scale high-quality 3D scene data with open-set annotations to introduce 1) a novel supervised fine-tuning dataset and 2) a new evaluation benchmark, focused on indoor scenes. Our Cubify Anything VQA (CA-VQA) data covers diverse spatial tasks including spatial relationship prediction, metric size and distance estimation, and 3D grounding. We show that CA-VQA enables us to train MM-Spatial, a strong generalist MLLM that also achieves state-of-the-art performance on 3D spatial understanding benchmarks, including our own. We show how incorporating metric depth and multi-view inputs (provided in CA-VQA) can further improve 3D understanding, and demonstrate that data alone allows our model to achieve depth perception capabilities comparable to dedicated monocular depth estimation models.

keyInsights: A 'generalist' MLLM can be trained to achieve state-of-the-art performance on 3D spatial understanding tasks without compromising its performance on other established MLLM benchmarks.
The incorporation of multi-view images and precise metric depth information (particularly via tool-use) substantially enhances an MLLM's 3D perception and metric estimation capabilities.

MLLMs, through supervised fine-tuning on curated data, can acquire intrinsic monocular metric depth estimation abilities that are comparable to or even exceed dedicated specialist depth estimation models.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) largely struggled with complex 3D spatial reasoning, limiting their application despite proficiency in 2D visual tasks.
The absence of robust 3D understanding impeded MLLM deployment in critical domains like robotics and augmented/virtual reality (AR/VR) that require precise 3D object perception.

Existing datasets for 3D spatial understanding in MLLMs often lacked comprehensive modalities like metric depth, suffered from limited scope, and were prone to language priors, hindering true visual reasoning evaluation.
results: MM-Spatial-3B demonstrated a substantial improvement on aggregated 'Spatial' category benchmarks (70.1% vs. 39.9% baseline) while maintaining competitive performance across general, knowledge, text-rich, and 2D referring/grounding tasks.
The model achieved state-of-the-art performance across all CA-VQA spatial tasks, including metric regression, outperforming much larger open-source and commercial models like GPT-4o.

Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning for depth prediction and utilizing ground-truth depth via tool-use consistently improved accuracy, with MM-Spatial's CoT approach for depth estimation rivaling dedicated monocular depth estimators.
solution: Developed `Cubify Anything VQA (CA-VQA)`, a high-quality, large-scale supervised fine-tuning dataset and benchmark for 3D object perception, leveraging ground-truth 3D scene data and incorporating multi-view images with diverse metric depth maps.

Trained `MM-Spatial`, a generalist MLLM based on the MM1.5 3B architecture, by integrating CA-VQA data through supervised fine-tuning, aimed at acquiring advanced 3D spatial understanding while preserving other general MLLM capabilities.

Implemented a data generation pipeline with automated QA pair creation and a 'blind filtering' strategy for the benchmark, effectively mitigating language priors to ensure visual reasoning capabilities were genuinely tested.
paper_summary.summary: Apple researchers developed MM-Spatial, a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) architecture, to significantly enhance 3D spatial understanding. The work introduces the Cubify Anything VQA (CA-VQA) dataset, enabling MM-Spatial to achieve state-of-the-art performance on 3D spatial tasks while retaining generalist MLLM capabilities, and demonstrating surprising intrinsic metric depth estimation abilities.

title: MM-Spatial: Exploring 3D Spatial Understanding in Multimodal LLMs

abstract:
Humans naturally understand 3D spatial relationships, enabling complex
reasoning like predicting collisions of vehicles from different directions.
Current large multimodal models (LMMs), however, lack of this capability of 3D
spatial reasoning. This limitation stems from the scarcity of 3D training data
and the bias in current model designs toward 2D data. In this paper, we
systematically study the impact of 3D-informed data, architecture, and training
setups, introducing SpatialLLM, a large multi-modal model with advanced 3D
spatial reasoning abilities. To address data limitations, we develop two types
of 3D-informed training datasets: (1) 3D-informed probing data focused on
object's 3D location and orientation, and (2) 3D-informed conversation data for
complex spatial relationships. Notably, we are the first to curate VQA data
that incorporate 3D orientation relationships on real images. Furthermore, we
systematically integrate these two types of training data with the
architectural and training designs of LMMs, providing a roadmap for optimal
design aimed at achieving superior 3D reasoning capabilities. Our SpatialLLM
advances machines toward highly capable 3D-informed reasoning, surpassing
GPT-4o performance by 8.7%. Our systematic empirical design and the resulting
findings offer valuable insights for future research in this direction. Our
project page is available at:
this https URL

keyInsights: A compound design systematically integrating 3D-informed data, architectural enhancements, and refined training strategies is crucial for building truly spatially-intelligent LMMs.

High-quality, human-annotated 3D orientation data is particularly vital for early multimodal alignment, and dedicated 3D-informed conversation data for instruction tuning yields the most substantial performance gains.

While an improved general-purpose LLM backbone contributes to better spatial reasoning, isolated pre-pretraining of the visual encoder with 3D-informed data can surprisingly degrade overall performance.
originalProblem: Existing Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) exhibit limited 3D spatial reasoning capabilities, struggling with understanding real-world 3D relationships like object orientation and precise distances.
This limitation stems from a scarcity of high-quality 3D training data, particularly for 3D object orientation, and architectural biases in current LMM designs optimized for 2D visual information.
Prior work on 3D spatial understanding in LMMs predominantly focused on 3D distance relationships, neglecting the critical aspect of 3D object orientation.
results: SpatialLLM achieved 62.7% accuracy on the SpatialVQA benchmark, outperforming GPT-4o (54.0%) by 8.7% and the best open-source spatial-centric models like SpatialVLM-13B (52.2%) by 10.5%.
The model demonstrated superior performance across all SpatialVQA sub-categories, including 3D Distance (86.3%), 3D Orientation (52.9%), and 3D Spatial Relationship (50.8%).

Ablation studies revealed 3D-informed data contributed a total of 13.7% performance gain, with 3D-informed instruction tuning providing +10.7% and 3D-informed multimodal alignment with human-annotated data adding a further +3%.
solution: A "compound 3D-informed design" is proposed, integrating enhancements across data curation, architectural components, and training strategies.
Two novel 3D-informed datasets are curated: 3D-informed probing data (including human-annotated 3D orientation from ImageNet3D) and 3D-informed conversation data for complex spatial relationships.
3D-informed data is strategically injected into both multimodal alignment and visual instruction tuning stages, alongside an upgraded visual encoder (CLIP+DINOv2) and LLM backbone (Llama3-8B).
A new benchmark, SpatialVQA, is introduced for comprehensive evaluation of 3D distance, orientation, and complex spatial reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Johns Hopkins University developed SpatialLLM, a compound 3D-informed design that systematically enhances Large Multimodal Models' 3D spatial reasoning capabilities. SpatialLLM achieved 62.7% accuracy on the new SpatialVQA benchmark, outperforming GPT-4o by 8.7% and the best open-source spatial-centric models by 10.5%.

title: SpatialLLM: A Compound 3D-Informed Design towards Spatially-Intelligent  Large Multimodal Models

abstract:
Multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) have rapidly advanced in visual
tasks, yet their spatial understanding remains limited to single images,
leaving them ill-suited for robotics and other real-world applications that
require multi-frame reasoning. In this paper, we propose a framework to equip
MLLMs with robust multi-frame spatial understanding by integrating depth
perception, visual correspondence, and dynamic perception. Central to our
approach is the MultiSPA dataset, a novel, large-scale collection of more than
27 million samples spanning diverse 3D and 4D scenes. Alongside MultiSPA, we
introduce a comprehensive benchmark that tests a wide spectrum of spatial tasks
under uniform metrics. Our resulting model, Multi-SpatialMLLM, achieves
significant gains over baselines and proprietary systems, demonstrating
scalable, generalizable multi-frame reasoning. We further observe multi-task
benefits and early indications of emergent capabilities in challenging
scenarios, and showcase how our model can serve as a multi-frame reward
annotator for robotics.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Multi-task training on diverse spatial reasoning tasks provides synergistic benefits and improved generalization
Model performance scales with both training data size and model capacity, particularly for challenging vector prediction tasks
Rigid body segmentation for point cloud clustering enhances training data diversity and model robustness
Multi-frame spatial understanding transfers effectively to external benchmarks while preserving original VQA capabilities
originalProblem: Current multi-modal large language models lack robust spatial understanding capabilities, particularly across multiple frames
Existing MLLMs cannot effectively reason about depth, visual correspondence, or dynamic changes between images
Scarcity of large-scale training data specifically designed for multi-frame spatial reasoning tasks
Limited evaluation frameworks for assessing multi-frame spatial understanding in vision-language models
results: Multi-SpatialMLLM achieves 36% performance improvement over InternVL2 baseline on MultiSPA benchmark
Model outperforms proprietary systems including Claude-3.5, Gemini 2.0, and GPT-4o on multi-frame spatial tasks
Demonstrates good generalization on BLINK benchmark while maintaining standard VQA proficiency
Shows preliminary emergent behavior in challenging spatial tasks and potential for robotics reward annotation applications
solution: Create MultiSPA dataset with 1.2 million instruction-following samples from Aria Digital Twin, Panoptic Studio, ScanNet, and TAPVid3D datasets
Develop data engine that samples image pairs with uniform overlap distribution and establishes pixel correspondences through backprojected point clouds
Design five core spatial reasoning tasks: depth perception, visual correspondence, camera movement, object movement, and object size perception
Fine-tune InternVL2 model using LoRA on MultiSPA dataset mixed with general instruction-following samples
paper_summary.summary: Meta AI and CUHK researchers develop Multi-SpatialMLLM by creating the MultiSPA dataset—1.2 million multi-frame instruction-following samples from real-world 3D/4D datasets—to train language models for spatial reasoning across image pairs, achieving 36% performance gains over InternVL2 baseline on tasks including depth perception, visual correspondence, camera movement detection, and object tracking while demonstrating transferable spatial understanding on external benchmarks and showing potential as a multi-frame reward annotator for robotics applications.

title: Multi-SpatialMLLM: Multi-Frame Spatial Understanding with Multi-Modal  Large Language Models

abstract: While Multimodal Large Language Models demonstrate impressive semantic capabilities, they often suffer from spatial blindness, struggling with fine-grained geometric reasoning and physical dynamics. Existing solutions typically rely on explicit 3D modalities or complex geometric scaffolding, which are limited by data scarcity and generalization challenges. In this work, we propose a paradigm shift by leveraging the implicit spatial prior within large-scale video generation models. We posit that to synthesize temporally coherent videos, these models inherently learn robust 3D structural priors and physical laws. We introduce VEGA-3D (Video Extracted Generative Awareness), a plug-and-play framework that repurposes a pre-trained video diffusion model as a Latent World Simulator. By extracting spatiotemporal features from intermediate noise levels and integrating them with semantic representations via a token-level adaptive gated fusion mechanism, we enrich MLLMs with dense geometric cues without explicit 3D supervision. Extensive experiments across 3D scene understanding, spatial reasoning, and embodied manipulation benchmarks demonstrate that our method outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, validating that generative priors provide a scalable foundation for physical-world understanding. Code is publicly available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Large-scale video generation models, particularly Transformer-based diffusion models (DiTs), implicitly learn robust 3D structural priors and physical laws from synthesizing temporally coherent videos.
A 'Multi-view Correspondence Score' can quantify a generative model's implicit 3D awareness, with higher scores strongly correlating with better downstream 3D task performance.
Moderately noisy latent representations and intermediate layers of the generative model's backbone yield the most informative 3D priors for fine-grained structural understanding.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) exhibit 'spatial blindness,' struggling with fine-grained geometric reasoning, precise spatial localization, and understanding physical dynamics in complex scenes.

Existing solutions rely on explicit 3D inputs (e.g., point clouds, depth maps) or complex geometric scaffolding, which are limited by scarce 3D annotated data, poor generalization, and high architectural complexity/training overhead.
results: VEGA-3D consistently improved performance across 3D scene understanding benchmarks (e.g., ScanRefer Acc@0.5 boosted from 51.7 to 56.2, SQA3D EM from 58.6 to 61.3), particularly in localization-centric tasks.

The framework generalized effectively to spatial reasoning tasks on VSI-Bench and enhanced robotic manipulation success rates on the LIBERO suite, demonstrating transferability of implicit physical knowledge.

Ablation studies confirmed that DiT-based architectures provide superior 3D priors, and the Adaptive Gated Fusion mechanism effectively balances semantic and geometric cues, outperforming other fusion strategies.
solution: A plug-and-play framework, VEGA-3D, repurposes a pre-trained video diffusion model as a 'Latent World Simulator' to extract dense geometric cues.
It activates the generative model's internal understanding by perturbing the input video's latent representation with noise and forcing it through a denoising process.
An Adaptive Gated Fusion mechanism dynamically integrates these extracted generative (structural) features with semantic features from a discriminative encoder, balancing their contributions.
paper_summary.summary: VEGA-3D integrates a pre-trained video diffusion model to provide dense geometric cues, improving fine-grained geometric reasoning and spatial understanding in Multimodal Large Language Models. The framework shows consistent performance gains in tasks like 3D visual grounding and robotic manipulation without explicit 3D supervision.

title: Generation Models Know Space: Unleashing Implicit 3D Priors for Scene Understanding

abstract: Recent progress in spatial reasoning with Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) increasingly leverages geometric priors from 3D encoders. However, most existing integration strategies remain passive: geometry is exposed as a global stream and fused in an indiscriminate manner, which often induces semantic-geometry misalignment and redundant signals. We propose GeoThinker, a framework that shifts the paradigm from passive fusion to active perception. Instead of feature mixing, GeoThinker enables the model to selectively retrieve geometric evidence conditioned on its internal reasoning demands. GeoThinker achieves this through Spatial-Grounded Fusion applied at carefully selected VLM layers, where semantic visual priors selectively query and integrate task-relevant geometry via frame-strict cross-attention, further calibrated by Importance Gating that biases per-frame attention toward task-relevant structures. Comprehensive evaluation results show that GeoThinker sets a new state-of-the-art in spatial intelligence, achieving a peak score of 72.6 on the VSI-Bench. Furthermore, GeoThinker demonstrates robust generalization and significantly improved spatial perception across complex downstream scenarios, including embodied referring and autonomous driving. Our results indicate that the ability to actively integrate spatial structures is essential for next-generation spatial intelligence. Code can be found at this https URL.

organization_info.name: Shenzhen campus of Sun Yet-sen University

keyInsights: Active, demand-driven integration of geometric cues, as opposed to passive feature mixing, significantly reduces noise and improves the relevance of spatial information for MLLMs.
The importance gating mechanism allows the model to autonomously identify and emphasize salient geometric features (e.g., object boundaries) without explicit object mask supervision.

Careful design, including frame-wise constraints and selective layer application of fusion, is crucial for maintaining semantic-geometry alignment and preventing the disruption of the MLLM's native understanding.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with robust 3D spatial reasoning due to 'passive fusion' strategies for geometric priors.
Existing methods often indiscriminately mix geometric features, leading to significant information redundancy and semantic-geometry misalignment.
The lack of task-conditioned selectivity in geometric integration limits MLLMs' ability to focus on pertinent spatial evidence, hindering performance and generalization.
results: GeoThinker achieved state-of-the-art performance across multiple spatial reasoning benchmarks, including an average score of 62.23 on a cross-benchmark comparison with the Qwen3VL-8B backbone.

The framework demonstrated robustness to language bias on VSI-Debiased and strong zero-shot extrapolation capabilities for longer video contexts, maintaining or improving performance with general video data mixtures.

Integration into downstream scenarios yielded practical gains: +1.66% average accuracy in embodied referring tasks and improvements in autonomous driving safety metrics, with a +3.7% increase in Drivable Area Compliance.
solution: GeoThinker employs a Spatial-Grounded Fusion (SGF) framework that actively integrates geometric cues from a 3D visual geometry encoder (VGGT) into the MLLM backbone.
An Importance Gating module dynamically regulates geometric information flow, prioritizing task-relevant regions by applying an additive attention bias based on predicted importance scores.
Frame-wise constraints ensure that semantic queries only attend to geometric cues from the corresponding image frame, preserving spatial consistency and preventing cross-image interference.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Sun Yet-sen University and Yinwang developed GeoThinker, an active geometry integration framework that enhances Multimodal Large Language Models' (MLLMs) spatial reasoning by selectively emphasizing task-relevant geometric cues. The system achieved state-of-the-art performance across various spatial reasoning benchmarks and improved real-world applications like autonomous driving, showing gains such as a +3.7% increase in Drivable Area Compliance.

title: Thinking with Geometry: Active Geometry Integration for Spatial Reasoning

abstract:
While multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have made groundbreaking
progress in embodied intelligence, they still face significant challenges in
spatial reasoning for complex long-horizon tasks. To address this gap, we
propose EmbodiedVSR (Embodied Visual Spatial Reasoning), a novel framework that
integrates dynamic scene graph-guided Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to
enhance spatial understanding for embodied agents. By explicitly constructing
structured knowledge representations through dynamic scene graphs, our method
enables zero-shot spatial reasoning without task-specific fine-tuning. This
approach not only disentangles intricate spatial relationships but also aligns
reasoning steps with actionable environmental dynamics. To rigorously evaluate
performance, we introduce the eSpatial-Benchmark, a comprehensive dataset
including real-world embodied scenarios with fine-grained spatial annotations
and adaptive task difficulty levels. Experiments demonstrate that our framework
significantly outperforms existing MLLM-based methods in accuracy and reasoning
coherence, particularly in long-horizon tasks requiring iterative environment
interaction. The results reveal the untapped potential of MLLMs for embodied
intelligence when equipped with structured, explainable reasoning mechanisms,
paving the way for more reliable deployment in real-world spatial applications.
The codes and datasets will be released soon.

organization_info.name: Beijing Innovation Center of Humanoid Robotics
Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: Explicitly modeling spatial relationships through dynamic scene graphs provides structured information, overcoming the limitations of implicit spatial knowledge in MLLMs.
Guiding chain-of-thought reasoning with scene graphs ensures that each reasoning step is physically grounded and geometrically consistent.
The integration of these components enables zero-shot generalization to novel environments and tasks, reducing the need for domain-specific training.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with implicit spatial knowledge, leading to geometrically inconsistent reasoning in complex 3D environments.
Existing MLLMs produce fragmented reasoning chains, causing error accumulation in long-horizon tasks requiring persistent environmental understanding.
Current models often lack a strong understanding of physical constraints and dynamics necessary for precise object manipulation in embodied AI applications.
results: EmbodiedVSR outperformed state-of-the-art baselines like GPT-4o and Claude 3 across all components of the new eSpatial-Benchmark.
The framework achieved substantial improvements in robotic-specific tasks, including an 18.4% gain in "Arm Feasibility" and a 6.7% gain in "Success Judgment" over GPT-4o on eSpatial-RoboMIND.
In real-world robotic manipulation, EmbodiedVSR demonstrated 100% accuracy in block assembly description and an 80% success rate in robot operations for complex reassembly tasks.
solution: Introduces EmbodiedVSR, a framework that generates and dynamically updates scene graphs to create explicit, structured representations of spatial relationships.
Integrates physics-constrained chain-of-thought reasoning, leveraging the dynamic scene graph to guide the MLLM's reasoning process and ensure physically plausible steps.
Developed a new comprehensive eSpatial-Benchmark to evaluate embodied spatial reasoning across general scenes, robotic scenarios, and structured assembly tasks.
paper_summary.summary: EmbodiedVSR enhances spatial reasoning in embodied AI by integrating dynamic scene graph generation with physics-constrained chain-of-thought reasoning. This approach achieves improved performance on a new eSpatial-Benchmark and demonstrates robust capabilities in real-world robotic assembly tasks.

title: EmbodiedVSR: Dynamic Scene Graph-Guided Chain-of-Thought Reasoning for  Visual Spatial Tasks

abstract: Previous research has investigated the application of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) in understanding 3D scenes by interpreting them as videos. These approaches generally depend on comprehensive 3D data inputs, such as point clouds or reconstructed Bird's-Eye View (BEV) maps. In our research, we advance this field by enhancing the capability of MLLMs to understand and reason in 3D spaces directly from video data, without the need for additional 3D input. We propose a novel and efficient method called the Video-3D Geometry Large Language Model (VG LLM). Our approach utilizes a 3D visual geometry encoder to extract 3D prior information from video sequences. This information is then integrated with visual tokens and input into the MLLM. Extensive experiments have shown that our method has achieved substantial improvements in various tasks related to 3D scene understanding and spatial reasoning, all directly learned from video sources. Impressively, our 4B model, which does not rely on explicit 3D data inputs, achieves competitive results compared to existing state-of-the-art methods, and even surpasses the Gemini-1.5-Pro in the VSI-Bench evaluations.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Robust 3D world understanding in MLLMs can be achieved directly from monocular videos by explicitly integrating latent 3D visual geometry priors, bypassing the need for explicit 3D data.
A straightforward additive fusion of 2D visual and 3D geometry features proves to be highly effective for enhancing spatial reasoning capabilities.

Incorporating raw latent 3D visual geometry features from a specialized encoder is more beneficial than relying on explicit predicted spatial information (e.g., camera poses, depth maps), which can be noisy.
originalProblem: Most MLLMs for 3D scene understanding rely heavily on dense 3D data inputs (e.g., point clouds, depth maps), which are costly and prone to errors in real-world acquisition.
Existing MLLMs process individual video frames as separate 2D tokens, failing to inherently capture crucial inter-frame 3D geometric information and consistent spatial relationships.
Inferring 3D structure from fragmented 2D representations is a complex and computationally intensive task, often requiring extensive supervision.
results: Achieved 41.6% IoU@0.25 on ScanRefer for 3D visual grounding (57.6% with proposal refinement), demonstrating performance competitive with methods using dense 3D inputs.
Set new state-of-the-art on VSI-Bench with the 8B model achieving 50.7% average accuracy, surpassing proprietary models like Gemini-1.5-Pro on complex spatial reasoning.

Demonstrated significant improvements in 3D video object detection (e.g., 8.7% F1 score gain for the 8B model), enhancing egocentric-allocentric transformation understanding and robustness across varying frame counts.
solution: VG LLM introduces a 3D visual geometry encoder that operates in parallel with a standard 2D visual encoder, integrating latent 3D geometry priors directly from video data.
It fuses 2D visual tokens and 3D visual geometry features using a simple yet effective additive strategy before feeding them into the MLLM backbone.

The model is trained with a multi-task learning approach on 3D scene understanding tasks (grounding, captioning, detection) and instruction-tuned on spatial reasoning datasets, all framed as text generation problems.
paper_summary.summary: VG LLM enhances Multimodal Large Language Models with a robust understanding of 3D spaces by directly leveraging video data and integrated 3D visual geometry priors. The framework achieves state-of-the-art performance on challenging spatial reasoning benchmarks and competitive results on 3D scene understanding tasks, without requiring explicit 3D inputs.

title: Learning from Videos for 3D World: Enhancing MLLMs with 3D Vision Geometry Priors

abstract: Though recent advances in vision-language models (VLMs) have achieved remarkable progress across a wide range of multimodal tasks, understanding 3D spatial relationships from limited views remains a significant challenge. Previous reasoning methods typically rely on pure text (e.g., topological cognitive maps) or on 2D visual cues. However, their limited representational capacity hinders performance in specific tasks that require 3D spatial imagination. To address this limitation, we propose 3DThinker, a framework that can effectively exploits the rich geometric information embedded within images while reasoning, like humans do. Our framework is the first to enable 3D mentaling during reasoning without any 3D prior input, and it does not rely on explicitly labeled 3D data for training. Specifically, our training consists of two stages. First, we perform supervised training to align the 3D latent generated by VLM while reasoning with that of a 3D foundation model (e.g., VGGT). Then, we optimize the entire reasoning trajectory solely based on outcome signals, thereby refining the underlying 3D mentaling. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks show that 3DThinker consistently outperforms strong baselines and offers a new perspective toward unifying 3D representations into multimodal reasoning. Our code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: VLMs can acquire an intrinsic '3D imagination' capability by learning to generate 3D-aware latent features directly from 2D images, bypassing the need for explicit 3D inputs or extensive 3D annotations.

Distilling geometric features from a pre-trained 3D foundation model into a VLM's internal representations, coupled with outcome-based reinforcement learning, effectively guides the model to learn implicit 3D understanding.
The framework offers enhanced interpretability by allowing approximate 3D point cloud reconstruction from the VLM's 3D latents, providing insight into the model's internal spatial reasoning process.
originalProblem: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) exhibit limited capabilities in comprehending complex 3D spatial relationships from sparse 2D images, which is critical for applications like embodied AI and autonomous driving.

Current VLM spatial reasoning methods often rely on restricted 2D cues, external auxiliary modalities (e.g., depth maps, point clouds), or labor-intensive dense 3D annotations, hindering intrinsic 3D understanding.

Existing approaches frequently introduce additional inference overhead or do not cultivate an inherent 3D imagination capacity within the VLM, limiting real-world applicability when explicit 3D data is unavailable.
results: 3DThinker achieved significant performance improvements on spatial reasoning benchmarks, with overall gains of 51.8% to 108.8% on MindCube-Tiny and 18.1% to 36.9% on Ego3D-Bench compared to base VLMs.

The approach consistently surpassed state-of-the-art spatial reasoning baselines across multiple benchmarks (e.g., VSI-Bench, SPBench, 3DSRBench), outperforming previous SOTA by 8.79% on 3DSRBench and improving over VILASR-7B by 33.7%.

3DThinker maintained or improved performance on general VLM benchmarks like POPE (2.91% improvement), indicating robustness and preservation of original 2D image understanding capabilities while enhancing 3D spatial intelligence.
solution: A two-stage framework, 3DThinker, is introduced that enables VLMs to form 3D mental representations during reasoning from limited 2D images.

Stage 1 involves supervised learning where 3D-aware latent features are generated by distilling geometric information from a pre-trained 3D foundation model (VGGT) into the VLM's hidden states via a projector, using both 3D alignment and textual coherence losses.

Stage 2 refines these 3D mental representations and the overall reasoning trajectory through reinforcement learning, using Group-relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a composite reward signal for 3D alignment, output format, and final answer correctness.
paper_summary.summary: The 3DThinker framework enables Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to develop intrinsic 3D spatial reasoning capabilities from limited 2D views, demonstrating performance gains ranging from 51.8% to 108.8% on MindCube-Tiny and 18.1% to 36.9% on Ego3D-Bench over baseline VLMs without requiring dense 3D annotations or external 3D priors during inference.

title: Think with 3D: Geometric Imagination Grounded Spatial Reasoning from Limited Views

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have made impressive progress in connecting vision and language, but they still struggle with spatial understanding and viewpoint-aware reasoning. Recent efforts aim to augment the input representations with geometric cues rather than explicitly teaching models to reason in 3D space. We introduce Loc3R-VLM, a framework that equips 2D Vision-Language Models with advanced 3D understanding capabilities from monocular video input. Inspired by human spatial cognition, Loc3R-VLM relies on two joint objectives: global layout reconstruction to build a holistic representation of the scene structure, and explicit situation modeling to anchor egocentric perspective. These objectives provide direct spatial supervision that grounds both perception and language in a 3D context. To ensure geometric consistency and metric-scale alignment, we leverage lightweight camera pose priors extracted from a pre-trained 3D foundation model. Loc3R-VLM achieves state-of-the-art performance in language-based localization and outperforms existing 2D- and video-based approaches on situated and general 3D question-answering benchmarks, demonstrating that our spatial supervision framework enables strong 3D understanding. Project page: this https URL

keyInsights: Explicitly supervising 2D VLMs with both global layout reconstruction and explicit situation modeling from monocular video can effectively instill robust 3D spatial understanding without needing 3D ground truth during inference.

Integrating lightweight camera pose priors from a 3D foundation model is crucial for providing metric-scale consistency, which significantly enhances absolute position estimation and numerical 3D reasoning tasks.

A joint training objective combining standard language modeling with spatial and situation-specific objectives fosters shared multimodal representations, bridging visual perception, spatial understanding, and embodied reasoning.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) lack robust 3D spatial understanding and viewpoint-aware reasoning, often processing information locally rather than integrating into a persistent global context.
Existing 3D-aware MLLM approaches frequently rely on precise 3D ground-truth data during inference, which is not consistently available in real-world scenarios.

Current models are primarily supervised on language or object-centric objectives, treating global scene understanding as a secondary outcome, leading to difficulties with viewpoint-dependent spatial relationships.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art performance in language-based localization on the SQA3D benchmark, surpassing the strongest baseline (View2Cap) by +25.2% in Acc@0.5m and +39.0% in Acc@1.0m for position estimation.

Demonstrated superior performance across various 3D Question Answering benchmarks (VSI-Bench, SQA3D, MSQA, ScanQA, Beacon3D), with substantial gains in viewpoint-dependent tasks, such as +36.1% in Relative Direction on VSI-Bench.

Ablation studies confirmed that global layout reconstruction and explicit situation modeling are key contributors to improved localization and 3D QA, with camera pose priors providing further metric-scale alignment for enhanced accuracy.
solution: Integrates per-frame latent camera tokens from a pre-trained 3D foundation model (CUT3R) into the VLM's visual stream to provide geometric grounding and metric-scale cues.

Introduces a global layout reconstruction objective that trains the model to associate vision tokens with corresponding 2D Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) coordinates, building a coherent internal map of the scene.
Employs an explicit situation modeling module with dedicated <Pos> and <Ori> tokens to estimate the agent's 2D location and orientation, enabling precise localization and viewpoint-aware reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: Loc3R-VLM augments 2D Vision-Language Models with advanced 3D spatial understanding and situational awareness using only monocular video input, achieving state-of-the-art performance in language-based localization and significantly improving 3D question answering across various benchmarks. It integrates global scene structure reconstruction and explicit situation modeling, outperforming prior methods by up to +39.0% in position accuracy and +36.1% in relative direction tasks.

title: Loc3R-VLM: Language-based Localization and 3D Reasoning with Vision-Language Models

abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have made significant advancements in vision understanding and reasoning. However, the autoregressive Transformer architecture used by MLLMs requries tokenization on input images, which limits their ability to accurately ground objects within the 2D image space. This raises an important question: how can sequential language tokens be improved to better ground objects in 2D spatial space for MLLMs? To address this, we present a spatial representation method for grounding objects, namely GETok, that integrates a specialized vocabulary of learnable tokens into MLLMs. GETok first uses grid tokens to partition the image plane into structured spatial anchors, and then exploits offset tokens to enable precise and iterative refinement of localization predictions. By embedding spatial relationships directly into tokens, GETok significantly advances MLLMs in native 2D space reasoning without modifying the autoregressive architecture. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GETok achieves superior performance over the state-of-the-art methods across various referring tasks in both supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning settings.
title: Grounding Everything in Tokens for Multimodal Large Language Models

abstract:
The rapid advancement of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) has
significantly impacted various multimodal tasks. However, these models face
challenges in tasks that require spatial understanding within 3D environments.
Efforts to enhance MLLMs, such as incorporating point cloud features, have been
made, yet a considerable gap remains between the models' learned
representations and the inherent complexity of 3D scenes. This discrepancy
largely stems from the training of MLLMs on predominantly 2D data, which
restricts their effectiveness in comprehending 3D spaces. To address this
issue, in this paper, we propose a novel generalist model, i.e., Video-3D LLM,
for 3D scene understanding. By treating 3D scenes as dynamic videos and
incorporating 3D position encoding into these representations, our Video-3D LLM
aligns video representations with real-world spatial contexts more accurately.
In addition, we have implemented a maximum coverage sampling technique to
optimize the trade-off between computational cost and performance. Extensive
experiments demonstrate that our model achieves state-of-the-art performance on
several 3D scene understanding benchmarks, including ScanRefer, Multi3DRefer,
Scan2Cap, ScanQA, and SQA3D.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Explicitly integrating global 3D coordinates into video representations is crucial for grounding spatio-temporal features in precise 3D space, outperforming implicit spatial learning or sparse 3D representations.
Pre-trained Video LLMs, when augmented with 3D-PE, offer strong priors that enable state-of-the-art 3D scene understanding with significantly less 3D training data.

Greedy maximum coverage sampling effectively balances comprehensive scene representation with computational efficiency, achieving comparable performance to uniform sampling at nearly half the inference time.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), primarily trained on 2D image data, struggle with robust 3D spatial understanding and reasoning.
Existing Video LLMs lack an explicit mechanism to encode precise 3D spatial information (global coordinates, relationships), limiting their effectiveness in detailed 3D tasks.
Prior 3D LLMs converting scenes to sparse 3D representations (point clouds/voxels) face a "data type discrepancy" and are hampered by scarce 3D training data.
results: Achieves state-of-the-art performance across five diverse 3D scene understanding benchmarks, including ScanRefer (visual grounding), Scan2Cap (dense captioning), and ScanQA (question answering).
Outperforms the prior state-of-the-art (LLaVA-3D) using only 26% of its 3D training data, demonstrating up to +4.1% Acc@0.25 on ScanRefer and +4.6 CIDEr@0.5IoU on Scan2Cap.
The maximum coverage sampling variant reduces inference time by approximately 50% (527ms vs. 1050ms) while maintaining performance comparable to uniform 32-frame sampling.
solution: Leverages a pre-trained Video LLM as a backbone, directly processing video streams captured from 3D scenes to preserve spatio-temporal information.
Introduces position-aware video representations by adding explicit 3D position encodings (derived from back-projected depth maps) to visual embeddings.
Implements a maximum coverage sampling strategy to efficiently select video frames that maximize 3D voxel coverage, balancing computational cost and scene completeness.
paper_summary.summary: Video-3D LLM, developed by researchers at The Chinese University of Hong Kong, improves 3D scene understanding by integrating explicit 3D position encoding directly into video representations. This generalist model achieves state-of-the-art performance across tasks like 3D visual grounding and dense captioning, demonstrating superior data efficiency with significantly less 3D training data.

title: Video-3D LLM: Learning Position-Aware Video Representation for 3D Scene  Understanding

abstract: Empowered by large-scale training, vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong image and video understanding, yet their ability to perform spatial reasoning in both static scenes and dynamic videos remains limited. Recent advances try to handle this limitation by injecting geometry tokens from pretrained 3D foundation models into VLMs. Nevertheless, we observe that naive token fusion followed by standard fine-tuning in this line of work often leaves such geometric cues underutilized for spatial reasoning, as VLMs tend to rely heavily on 2D visual cues. In this paper, we propose GeoSR, a framework designed to make geometry matter by encouraging VLMs to actively reason with geometry tokens. GeoSR introduces two key components: (1) Geometry-Unleashing Masking, which strategically masks portions of 2D vision tokens during training to weaken non-geometric shortcuts and force the model to consult geometry tokens for spatial reasoning; and (2) Geometry-Guided Fusion, a gated routing mechanism that adaptively amplifies geometry token contributions in regions where geometric evidence is critical. Together, these designs unleash the potential of geometry tokens for spatial reasoning tasks. Extensive experiments on both static and dynamic spatial reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that GeoSR consistently outperforms prior methods and establishes new state-of-the-art performance by effectively leveraging geometric information. The project page is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Simply providing geometry tokens to VLMs is insufficient; specific mechanisms are required to compel models to actively and effectively utilize this geometric information.
VLMs tend to rely on appearance-driven shortcuts from 2D visual cues, treating geometry tokens as dispensable, which limits spatial reasoning capabilities.
A combination of training-time appearance masking and an adaptive, context-aware fusion mechanism is essential for unlocking the full potential of geometric priors in VLMs.
originalProblem: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate brittleness in complex spatial reasoning tasks, such as understanding 3D relationships, distances, and directions.
Existing methods for injecting 3D geometric information (geometry tokens) into VLMs often lead to underutilization, with models defaulting to 2D appearance-driven shortcuts.
Naive token fusion and standard fine-tuning approaches can result in marginal performance gains or even a decrease in accuracy, particularly in dynamic scenarios.
results: GeoSR achieved new state-of-the-art performance, with 51.9% average accuracy on the VSI-Bench (static) and 66.1% on the DSR-Bench (dynamic) spatial reasoning datasets.
It significantly outperformed strong baselines, including proprietary models like GPT-4o and Gemini-1.5 Pro, and specialized spatial reasoning models such as VG-LLM and GSM.

Ablation studies confirmed that both Geometry-Unleashing Masking and Geometry-Guided Fusion are critical for GeoSR's performance, demonstrating that removing either component leads to noticeable accuracy drops.
solution: GeoSR introduces Geometry-Unleashing Masking, which suppresses 2D appearance-driven shortcuts during training, forcing the VLM to consult geometry tokens for spatial reasoning.
A Geometry-Guided Fusion module adaptively controls the contribution of geometric evidence to each fused token, ensuring geometry dominates when contextually relevant.

Distinct masking and fusion strategies are employed for static and dynamic spatial reasoning, with dynamic masking being guided by question-relevant geometric evidence and dynamic fusion involving redistribution of compact geometry.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at the National University of Singapore developed GeoSR, a framework that compels Vision-Language Models (VLMs) to effectively integrate geometric information for spatial reasoning. It established new state-of-the-art accuracy on both static (51.9% on VSI-Bench) and dynamic (66.1% on DSR-Bench) spatial reasoning benchmarks, outperforming strong proprietary and specialized models.

title: Make Geometry Matter for Spatial Reasoning

abstract: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in vision-language tasks, but they continue to struggle with spatial understanding. Existing spatial MLLMs often rely on explicit 3D inputs or architecture-specific modifications, and remain constrained by large-scale datasets or sparse supervision. To address these limitations, we introduce SpatialThinker, a 3D-aware MLLM trained with RL to integrate structured spatial grounding with multi-step reasoning. The model simulates human-like spatial perception by constructing a scene graph of task-relevant objects and spatial relations, and reasoning towards an answer via dense spatial rewards. SpatialThinker consists of two key contributions: (1) a data synthesis pipeline that generates STVQA-7K, a high-quality spatial VQA dataset, and (2) online RL with a multi-objective dense spatial reward enforcing spatial grounding. SpatialThinker-7B outperforms supervised fine-tuning and the sparse RL baseline on spatial understanding and real-world VQA benchmarks, nearly doubling the base-model gain compared to sparse RL, and surpassing GPT-4o. These results showcase the effectiveness of combining spatial supervision with reward-aligned reasoning in enabling robust 3D spatial understanding with limited data and advancing MLLMs towards human-level visual reasoning.

keyInsights: Dense, structured spatial rewards with lexicographic gating are essential for MLLMs to learn robust 3D spatial reasoning, effectively preventing reward hacking and guiding complex, multi-step processes.

Effective spatial grounding can be achieved with a comparatively small, high-quality synthetic dataset (7K samples) when combined with a sophisticated reward mechanism, reducing reliance on massive data collections.

Reinforcement learning with rich, dense feedback significantly enhances generalization and transferability of spatial priors, improving performance on both synthetic spatial tasks and diverse real-world visual reasoning benchmarks.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) consistently struggle with robust 3D spatial understanding, failing to reliably comprehend complex 3D relationships, depth, and precise object localization.
Existing methods for enhancing spatial reasoning in MLLMs typically require millions of training samples or explicit 3D inputs, leading to high computational costs and limited scalability.
Reinforcement learning approaches often use sparse rewards (e.g., final answer correctness), which provide insufficient guidance for learning intricate, multi-step spatial reasoning processes.
results: SPATIALTHINKER-7B attained an average accuracy of 78.2% on CV-Bench and outperformed GPT-4o by 12.1% on the 3DSRBench, demonstrating superior 3D spatial reasoning.

The model consistently surpassed supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and sparse-reward RL baselines across 12 spatial benchmarks, showing a +6.0% accuracy gain over SFT and doubling the benefit of RL training (1.8x) compared to sparse rewards.

Despite being trained on only 7K synthetic samples, SPATIALTHINKER-7B achieved state-of-the-art open-source performance and robust generalization, with an 81.7% score on VStarBench and competitive results on other real-world VQA tasks.
solution: A synthetic, high-quality STVQA-7K dataset was created from human-annotated scene graphs, enriched with 34 detailed spatial relations and filtered for consistency, to provide data-efficient spatial supervision.

A multi-objective, lexicographically gated dense spatial reward function was designed, combining format, accuracy, object count, and Complete IoU (CIoU) based spatial alignment to provide fine-grained guidance.

Online Group-Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) was employed to train the MLLM, iteratively refining its autoregressive policy by generating structured reasoning steps and answers, with all model parameters updated.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the University of Oxford and UC Santa Cruz developed SPATIALTHINKER, a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) that utilizes a multi-objective dense spatial reward function within an online reinforcement learning framework. Trained on a compact 7K synthetic dataset, this model achieved state-of-the-art open-source performance on 3D spatial reasoning benchmarks and improved generalization on real-world visual question answering tasks.

title: SpatialThinker: Reinforcing 3D Reasoning in Multimodal LLMs via Spatial Rewards

abstract: Recent advances in scene understanding have leveraged multimodal large language models (MLLMs) for 3D reasoning by capitalizing on their strong 2D pretraining. However, the lack of explicit 3D data during MLLM pretraining limits 3D representation capability. In this paper, we investigate the 3D-awareness of MLLMs by evaluating multi-view correspondence and reveal a strong positive correlation between the quality of 3D-aware representation and downstream task performance. Motivated by this, we propose 3DRS, a framework that enhances MLLM 3D representation learning by introducing supervision from pretrained 3D foundation models. Our approach aligns MLLM visual features with rich 3D knowledge distilled from 3D models, effectively improving scene understanding. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks and MLLMs -- including visual grounding, captioning, and question answering -- demonstrate consistent performance gains. Project page: this https URL

keyInsights: A robust positive correlation exists between the quality of an MLLM's 3D-aware representations (quantified by multi-view correspondence scores) and its performance on downstream 3D scene understanding tasks.

3D foundation models, pretrained on diverse geometric tasks, are significantly more effective teachers for imparting 3D-aware representations to MLLMs compared to 2D foundation models or simpler correspondence-based supervision.
Distilling 3D knowledge into the deeper layers of MLLMs, particularly the last visual layer, yields the most substantial performance improvements across various tasks.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), primarily pretrained on 2D data, lack explicit exposure to 3D information, limiting their ability to capture intrinsic 3D attributes and spatial structures essential for robust scene understanding.

Existing MLLM-based methods for 3D understanding either rely on scarce 3D-text datasets or employ superficial 3D prior injection (e.g., positional embeddings), without systematically enhancing intrinsic 3D-aware representation learning.
A clear method to evaluate and improve the quality of 3D-aware representations learned by MLLMs for downstream scene understanding tasks was largely undeveloped.
results: 3DRS improved LLaVA-Next-Video's ScanRefer Acc@0.25 from 58.1 to 62.9 and ScanQA CIDEr from 102.1 to 104.8, demonstrating superior performance over state-of-the-art 3D generalist models.
The framework consistently enhanced the performance of diverse MLLM architectures (LLaVA-Next-Video, LLaVA-OneVision, Qwen2-VL) across all evaluated 3D scene understanding benchmarks.

3DRS achieved competitive open-source results on the VSI-Bench for visual-based spatial understanding tasks (e.g., object count, absolute distance, room size), performing comparably to proprietary models like GPT-4o.
solution: The paper proposes 3DRS (3D-Aware Representation Supervision), a framework that enhances MLLMs' 3D-aware representation learning through knowledge distillation.
It utilizes pretrained 3D foundation models (e.g., FLARE, VGGT) as teachers to generate per-pixel 3D features encoding rich geometric properties from multi-view images.

A feature alignment loss (L_align) distills these 3D-aware features from the teacher into the MLLM's visual features, integrated with the standard cross-entropy loss for autoregressive text generation.
paper_summary.summary: This research introduces 3DRS, a framework that leverages knowledge distillation from 3D foundation models to supervise multimodal large language model (MLLM) visual features, addressing their inherent lack of 3D understanding due to 2D pretraining. The approach consistently improves MLLM performance across various 3D scene understanding tasks such as visual grounding, captioning, and question answering, achieving notable gains over existing methods.

title: 3DRS: MLLMs Need 3D-Aware Representation Supervision for Scene Understanding

abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have been
proven to excel at multiple tasks, such as commonsense reasoning. Powerful as
these models can be, they are not grounded in the 3D physical world, which
involves richer concepts such as spatial relationships, affordances, physics,
layout, and so on. In this work, we propose to inject the 3D world into large
language models and introduce a whole new family of 3D-LLMs. Specifically,
3D-LLMs can take 3D point clouds and their features as input and perform a
diverse set of 3D-related tasks, including captioning, dense captioning, 3D
question answering, task decomposition, 3D grounding, 3D-assisted dialog,
navigation, and so on. Using three types of prompting mechanisms that we
design, we are able to collect over 300k 3D-language data covering these tasks.
To efficiently train 3D-LLMs, we first utilize a 3D feature extractor that
obtains 3D features from rendered multi- view images. Then, we use 2D VLMs as
our backbones to train our 3D-LLMs. By introducing a 3D localization mechanism,
3D-LLMs can better capture 3D spatial information. Experiments on ScanQA show
that our model outperforms state-of-the-art baselines by a large margin (e.g.,
the BLEU-1 score surpasses state-of-the-art score by 9%). Furthermore,
experiments on our held-in datasets for 3D captioning, task composition, and
3D-assisted dialogue show that our model outperforms 2D VLMs. Qualitative
examples also show that our model could perform more tasks beyond the scope of
existing LLMs and VLMs. Project Page: : https://vis-www.cs.umass.edu/3dllm/.

keyInsights: Holistic understanding of 3D scenes can be achieved directly from raw 3D features, outperforming methods reliant on explicit object detection.
Leveraging pre-trained 2D vision-language models and generating large-scale 3D-language data via advanced prompting significantly mitigates the scarcity of paired 3D-language datasets.
Injecting explicit spatial context through position embeddings for 3D features and dedicated location tokens in the language model's vocabulary enables robust 3D localization and reasoning.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs) lack grounding in the 3D physical world, limiting their understanding of spatial relationships, object affordances, and scene layouts.
Existing 3D-language models are typically task-specific, lacking the generalizability of foundation models to handle diverse 3D-related tasks comprehensively.
There is a severe scarcity of high-quality, diverse 3D-language paired data, which hinders the training of large-scale 3D foundation models.
results: 3D-LLMs significantly outperformed state-of-the-art baselines on the ScanQA dataset, achieving an approximate 9% increase in BLEU-1 score on the validation set and 7% on the test set.
The models demonstrated superior performance over 2D VLMs and language-only LLMs on custom-generated datasets for 3D captioning, 3D-assisted dialogue, and task decomposition.

For 3D grounding on ScanRefer, 3D-LLMs achieved an average distance between predicted and ground-truth bounding box centers of around 1.03 to 1.1 meters without relying on explicit object proposal modules.
solution: A novel data generation pipeline leverages LLMs like ChatGPT and 2D VLMs to create over 300,000 diverse 3D-language data points from existing 3D datasets such as Objaverse, ScanNet, and HM3D.

Meaningful 3D features are extracted by rendering 3D scenes from multiple views and robustly constructing 3D features from pre-trained 2D image encoders using methods like direct reconstruction, feature fusion, or neural fields.

3D-LLMs are trained on pre-trained 2D VLM backbones (e.g., Flamingo, BLIP-2), with a novel 3D localization mechanism that augments 3D features with position embeddings and extends LLM vocabularies with discrete location tokens.
paper_summary.summary: The 3D-LLM framework from a collaboration including MIT and UMass Amherst enables large language models to understand and reason about the 3D physical world. It achieves this by generating large-scale 3D-language data and deriving 3D features from multi-view 2D images, demonstrating improved performance across tasks like 3D question answering and object grounding compared to prior methods.

title: 3D-LLM: Injecting the 3D World into Large Language Models

abstract: While Vision-Language Models (VLMs) exhibit exceptional 2D visual understanding, their ability to comprehend and reason about 3D space--a cornerstone of spatial intelligence--remains superficial. Current methodologies attempt to bridge this domain gap either by relying on explicit 3D modalities or by augmenting VLMs with partial, view-conditioned geometric priors. However, such approaches hinder scalability and ultimately burden the language model with the ill-posed task of implicitly reconstructing holistic 3D geometry from sparse cues. In this paper, we argue that spatial intelligence can emerge inherently from 2D vision alone, rather than being imposed via explicit spatial instruction tuning. To this end, we introduce Spa3R, a self-supervised framework that learns a unified, view-invariant spatial representation directly from unposed multi-view images. Spa3R is built upon the proposed Predictive Spatial Field Modeling (PSFM) paradigm, where Spa3R learns to synthesize feature fields for arbitrary unseen views conditioned on a compact latent representation, thereby internalizing a holistic and coherent understanding of the underlying 3D scene. We further integrate the pre-trained Spa3R Encoder into existing VLMs via a lightweight adapter to form Spa3-VLM, effectively grounding language reasoning in a global spatial context. Experiments on the challenging VSI-Bench demonstrate that Spa3-VLM achieves state-of-the-art accuracy of 58.6% on 3D VQA, significantly outperforming prior methods. These results highlight PSFM as a scalable path toward advancing spatial intelligence. Code is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Holistic, view-invariant 3D scene understanding can emerge inherently through a predictive modeling paradigm from 2D multi-view data, offering a scalable alternative to explicit 3D data or fragmented priors.
Decoupling the learning of robust 3D spatial representations from the subsequent language reasoning task provides a more effective and scalable foundation for 3D grounding in VLMs.
The combination of both geometric and semantic feature reconstruction targets during pre-training is crucial for developing comprehensive and robust spatial representations within the model.
originalProblem: Current Vision-Language Models (VLMs) exhibit superficial comprehension and reasoning abilities in 3D space despite their strengths in 2D visual tasks.

Existing methods for imparting 3D spatial awareness often rely on expensive explicit 3D data or provide only partial, view-conditioned geometric priors, which leads to inefficient and suboptimal spatial reasoning.

Scaling VLMs with extensive multi-view data paired with spatial Question-Answer (QA) annotations is data-intensive and forces the language model to inefficiently learn fundamental spatial understanding from scratch.
results: Achieved state-of-the-art average accuracy of 58.6% on the VSI-Bench, surpassing the previous best (Cambrian-S-3B) by 1.3 percentage points, demonstrating superior visual-spatial intelligence from video inputs.
Exhibited strong cross-domain generalization, outperforming baselines on diverse spatial reasoning benchmarks such as SPAR-Bench, where Spa3-VLM scored 58.4% compared to SpaceR-7B's 37.6%.

Ablation studies confirmed the effectiveness of Spa3R's unified spatial representation (+3.5% over partial priors), the synergy of geometric and semantic reconstruction targets, and the crucial role of the Residual Cross-Attention Adapter (+7.5% over naive token appending).
solution: Introduces Predictive Spatial Field Modeling (PSFM), a self-supervised framework that learns a unified, view-invariant spatial representation from unposed multi-view 2D images by synthesizing feature fields for arbitrary, unseen views.

Develops a specialized Spa3R encoder-decoder architecture featuring an Asymmetric View Aggregator, a Transformer-based Spa3R Encoder, and a Spa3R Decoder that utilizes Relative 3D Positional Encoding (PRoPE) for target view feature synthesis.

Integrates the pre-trained Spa3R Encoder into a base VLM (Qwen2.5-VL) via a lightweight Residual Cross-Attention Adapter, enabling the VLM to query the holistic spatial latent representation without compromising its original capabilities.
paper_summary.summary: Spa3R introduces Predictive Spatial Field Modeling (PSFM), a self-supervised deep learning framework that enables Vision-Language Models to learn holistic, view-invariant 3D spatial representations directly from unposed multi-view 2D images. This approach achieved a state-of-the-art average accuracy of 58.6% on the VSI-Bench, outperforming previous spatially-grounded baselines by 1.3 percentage points, and showed strong generalization across various spatial reasoning benchmarks.

title: Spa3R: Predictive Spatial Field Modeling for 3D Visual Reasoning

abstract: Despite the remarkable success of large-scale pre-trained image representation models (i.e., vision encoders) across various vision tasks, they are predominantly trained on 2D image data and therefore often fail to capture 3D spatial relationships between objects and backgrounds in the real world, constraining their effectiveness in many downstream applications. To address this, we propose SpatialBoost, a scalable framework that enhances the spatial awareness of existing pre-trained vision encoders by injecting 3D spatial knowledge expressed in linguistic descriptions. The core idea involves converting dense 3D spatial information from 2D images into linguistic expressions, which is then used to inject such spatial knowledge into vision encoders through a Large Language Model (LLM). To this end, we adopt a multi-turn Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning process that progressively incorporates dense spatial knowledge and builds hierarchical spatial understanding. To validate effectiveness, we adapt SpatialBoost to state-of-the-art vision encoders such as DINOv3, and evaluate its performance gains on a wide range of benchmarks requiring both 3D perception and general vision abilities. For instance, SpatialBoost improves DINOv3 performance from 55.9 to 59.7 mIoU on ADE20K, achieving state-of-the-art performance with 3.8% gain over the pre-trained DINOv3.

keyInsights: Language can effectively convey dense and hierarchical 3D spatial knowledge, serving as a scalable supervision signal for enhancing vision encoders.
A multi-turn Chain-of-Thought reasoning process, ranging from pixel-level to object-level and scene-level spatial queries, facilitates comprehensive 3D understanding.
The dual-channel attention mechanism provides an efficient method to inject new knowledge into pre-trained models without catastrophic forgetting of existing representations.
originalProblem: Vision encoders, primarily trained on 2D data, often lack comprehensive understanding of 3D spatial relationships between objects and environments.
This deficiency limits their effectiveness in applications requiring 3D spatial awareness, such as 3D scene understanding, monocular depth prediction, and robotic control.
Existing methods for 3D spatial learning, like multi-view data collection or full multimodal model re-training, are often resource-intensive and lack scalability for generalizable pre-training.
