results: Improved DINOv3's semantic segmentation mIoU on ADE20K by 3.8% (from 55.9% to 59.7% with linear probing) and its monocular depth estimation RMSE on NYUd from 0.31 to 0.25.
Enhanced vision-based robot learning, with DINOv3's average score on CortexBench increasing by 8.0 points (from 72.8 to 80.8).
Achieved significant gains in complex 3D-centric tasks, including a 3.5%p improvement in DINOv3's SQA3D BLEU-1 score and an increase in Registration Recall at 0.05m from 86.9% to 97.5%.
solution: A three-stage training framework (Feature Alignment, Visual Instruction Tuning, Vision Encoder Fine-tuning) adapts existing pre-trained vision encoders.
Dense 3D spatial information from 2D images is converted into structured linguistic expressions using specialized vision models and a Large Language Model (LLM).
A dual-channel attention mechanism is integrated during vision encoder fine-tuning to acquire new spatial knowledge while preserving pre-trained general visual capabilities.
paper_summary.summary: A framework developed at KAIST, RLWRLD, and NAVER Cloud enhances existing vision encoders with 3D spatial understanding by leveraging linguistic descriptions generated from visual data through a Large Language Model and a dual-channel attention mechanism. The approach improved DINOv3's semantic segmentation mIoU on ADE20K by 3.8% and its average robot task score on CortexBench by 8.0 points.

title: SpatialBoost: Enhancing Visual Representation through Language-Guided Reasoning

abstract: Spatial intelligence is a critical frontier for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), empowering them to comprehend the physical world. Drawing inspiration from human perception mechanisms, prior studies attempt to construct a spatial understanding via grid-based cognitive maps. However, current grid-based map methods rely on discretized representations, which limit the model's ability in fine-grained spatial reasoning. To overcome this limitation, we propose Video2Layout, a framework for reconstructing metric-grounded spatial layouts from video. The framework uses continuous object boundary coordinates to enable quantitative spatial computation, which effectively reduces ambiguity in natural language descriptions of spatial relationships. Specifically, our method comprises two stages. First, in supervised fine-tuning stage, we construct a high-quality dataset from the AI2THOR simulator, which enables the model to learn the mapping from visual inputs to precise boundary coordinates. Subsequently, a reinforcement fine-tuning stage enhances the model's real-world generalization capabilities. Based on the above framework, we investigate factors that affect cognitive map accuracy and quantify its relationship with task performance. Evaluated on mainstream spatial reasoning benchmarks, our model, V2LO-7B, achieves an average improvement of 3.24\% over the model trained on grid maps, validating the superiority of our method.
title: Video2Layout: Recall and Reconstruct Metric-Grounded Cognitive Map for Spatial Reasoning

abstract: Current multimodal large language models (MLLMs) still face significant challenges in complex visual tasks (e.g., spatial understanding, fine-grained perception). Prior methods have tried to incorporate visual reasoning, however, they fail to leverage attention correction with spatial cues to iteratively refine their focus on prompt-relevant regions. In this paper, we introduce SIFThinker, a spatially-aware "think-with-images" framework that mimics human visual perception. Specifically, SIFThinker enables attention correcting and image region focusing by interleaving depth-enhanced bounding boxes and natural language. Our contributions are twofold: First, we introduce a reverse-expansion-forward-inference strategy that facilitates the generation of interleaved image-text chains of thought for process-level supervision, which in turn leads to the construction of the SIF-50K dataset. Besides, we propose GRPO-SIF, a reinforced training paradigm that integrates depth-informed visual grounding into a unified reasoning pipeline, teaching the model to dynamically correct and focus on prompt-relevant regions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that SIFThinker outperforms state-of-the-art methods in spatial understanding and fine-grained visual perception, while maintaining strong general capabilities, highlighting the effectiveness of our method. Code: this https URL.

keyInsights: The inherent integration of dynamic, human-like attention with explicit 3D spatial awareness significantly boosts MLLMs' capabilities for complex visual reasoning and grounding.
Process-level supervision using interleaved image-text chains of thought with depth-enhanced bounding boxes is critical for training MLLMs to perform intrinsic, fine-grained visual focus.

A reinforcement learning paradigm with specifically designed reward functions, particularly those encouraging iterative correction and depth consistency, effectively refines a model's visual grounding and spatial understanding.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with complex visual tasks that require fine-grained perception and robust spatial understanding, often processing images uniformly.
Current MLLMs lack the dynamic attention shifts and inherent 3D spatial awareness characteristic of human visual perception, hindering accurate interpretation of 2D projections.
Existing 'think-with-images' methods often rely on fragmented reasoning steps, external tools, or outcome-based supervision, leading to less coherent and intrinsic multimodal reasoning.
results: SIFThinker achieved state-of-the-art performance on SpatialBench, surpassing SpatialBot by 7.82% and SSR by 11.17%, and outperformed ChatGPT-o3 by 8.01% on the SAT (Static) benchmark.
The framework demonstrated superior visual perception, outperforming SEAL by 5.75% on V*Bench-Attribute and showing comparable overall performance to ChatGPT-o3 on VisCoT s.

Improved grounding capabilities were observed in Referring Expression Comprehension and Open-Vocabulary Detection, with a 45.9% gain over VisRL on OVDEval, while maintaining strong performance on general VLM benchmarks (e.g., ~4% improvement on MMBench).
solution: SIFThinker introduces a unified framework that merges dynamic visual perception, including attention correction and region focusing, with 3D spatial awareness via explicit depth information.

A novel dataset, SIF-50K, was created using a reverse-expansion–forward-inference strategy to provide process-level supervision for spatially-aware visual focus through interleaved image-text chains of thought.

The model is trained in two stages: supervised fine-tuning for structured reasoning, followed by reinforcement learning (GRPO-SIF) with a composite reward function that includes reasoning format, progressive answer accuracy, correction-enhanced grounding via Hierarchical IoU, and depth consistency.
paper_summary.summary: The SIFThinker framework enhances multimodal large language models by intrinsically integrating dynamic visual attention with 3D spatial awareness, mimicking human perception. Researchers from Tsinghua University and ByteDance developed this approach, which achieved state-of-the-art results across spatial intelligence, fine-grained visual perception, and grounding benchmarks, including an 8.01% improvement over ChatGPT-o3 on the SAT (Static) benchmark.

title: SIFThinker: Spatially-Aware Image Focus for Visual Reasoning

abstract: Achieving human-like spatial intelligence for vision-language models (VLMs) requires inferring 3D structures from 2D observations, recognizing object properties and relations in 3D space, and performing high-level spatial reasoning. In this paper, we propose a principled hierarchical framework that decomposes the learning of 3D spatial understanding in VLMs into four progressively complex levels, from geometric perception to abstract spatial reasoning. Guided by this framework, we construct an automated pipeline that processes approximately 5M images with over 45M objects to generate 3D spatial VQA pairs across diverse tasks and scenes for VLM supervised fine-tuning. We also develop an RGB-D VLM incorporating metric-scale point maps as auxiliary inputs to further enhance spatial understanding. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple spatial understanding and reasoning benchmarks, surpassing specialized spatial models and large proprietary systems such as Gemini-2.5-pro and GPT-5. Moreover, our analysis reveals clear dependencies among hierarchical task levels, offering new insights into how multi-level task design facilitates the emergence of 3D spatial intelligence.

keyInsights: Lower-level spatial tasks (geometric perception and object-level understanding) implicitly contribute to the capture of richer spatial information, benefiting the performance of higher-level relational and abstract reasoning tasks.
Integrating explicit metric-scale 3D point maps as auxiliary input is more effective for enhancing VLM spatial understanding, especially for quantitative tasks, compared to using relative depth maps.
The proposed hierarchical training approach enables VLMs to acquire comprehensive 3D spatial intelligence without compromising their general visual understanding capabilities.
originalProblem: Vision-language models (VLMs) struggle with inferring intricate 3D structures, recognizing object properties and relationships, and performing high-level spatial reasoning from standard 2D visual inputs.

A unified and systematic task design that holistically captures the diverse cognitive levels of 3D spatial intelligence is absent, and the interdependencies between different spatial reasoning skills are not well-defined.

The acquisition of large-scale, diverse, and accurately 3D-grounded data remains a significant hurdle, as existing 3D datasets are often limited in scale or scope, and web data lacks explicit 3D supervision.
results: The HiSpatial-3B model, utilizing RGB-XYZ input, achieved state-of-the-art performance on various spatial reasoning benchmarks, surpassing proprietary models like GPT-4o and GPT-5 in quantitative spatial VQA.
On qualitative spatial VQA benchmarks, HiSpatial-3B achieved 80.71% on EmbSpatial and 86.18% on RoboSpatial, demonstrating comprehensive spatial understanding across multiple levels.

Evaluation on general VQA benchmarks (e.g., MMBench, POPE) showed that HiSpatial-3B not only retained but generally surpassed its base model's performance, indicating that spatial fine-tuning did not negatively impact general visual understanding.
solution: A principled hierarchical framework was formulated, decomposing 3D spatial understanding into four progressively complex levels: Basic Geometric Perception, Object-level, Inter-object Relational, and Abstract Spatial Reasoning.

An automated pipeline was developed to construct a large-scale and diverse 3D spatial VQA dataset, synthesizing over 2 billion QA pairs from approximately 5 million images, covering all hierarchical tasks.
An RGB-D VLM architecture was developed, built on PaliGemma-2, which effectively integrates metric-scale 3D point maps as auxiliary inputs to enhance spatial grounding.
paper_summary.summary: HiSpatial introduces a framework and RGB-D VLM designed to foster comprehensive hierarchical 3D spatial understanding in vision-language models from 2D inputs. The model leverages a vast, automatically generated dataset of over 2 billion spatial QA pairs, achieving state-of-the-art performance across multiple spatial reasoning benchmarks, including outperforming GPT-4o and GPT-5 in quantitative spatial VQA.

title: HiSpatial: Taming Hierarchical 3D Spatial Understanding in Vision-Language Models

abstract: Spatial reasoning, the ability to understand and interpret the 3D structure of the world, is a critical yet underdeveloped capability in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). Current methods predominantly rely on verbal descriptive tuning, which suffers from visual illiteracy, i.e., they learn spatial concepts through textual symbols alone, devoid of connection to their visual manifestations. To bridge this gap, this paper introduces MILO, an Implicit spatIaL wOrld modeling paradigm that simulates human-like spatial imagination. MILO integrates a visual generator to provide geometry-aware feedback, thereby implicitly grounding the MLLM's symbolic reasoning in perceptual experience. Complementing this paradigm, we propose RePE (Relative Positional Encoding), a novel encoding scheme that captures relative camera-pose transformations, offering superior performance over absolute coordinate systems. To support the training, we construct GeoGen, a large-scale Geometry-aware Generative dataset with approximately 2,241 videos and 67,827 observation-action-outcome triplets. Experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly enhances spatial reasoning capabilities across multiple baselines and benchmarks, offering a more holistic understanding of 3D space.

keyInsights: Visual generative tuning, by supervising MLLMs on the visual outcomes of geometric transformations, enables models to implicitly internalize 3D world dynamics and ground symbolic reasoning in perceptual feedback.

Focusing on relative camera-pose variations with RePE provides a more robust and generalizable geometric representation than absolute 3D coordinate systems, improving performance across diverse scenes.
A dedicated dataset with geometry-aware generative tasks, like GeoGen, is essential to provide the necessary visual feedback for MLLMs to build an internal model of the 3D world.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with "visual illiteracy," learning spatial concepts purely through textual symbols without robust visual grounding in 3D environments.
Existing verbal descriptive tuning approaches often result in MLLMs failing to correctly attend to relevant visual regions when answering spatial queries.
Absolute 3D positional encodings used in MLLMs are sensitive to arbitrary global coordinate system choices, limiting their generalization across diverse camera setups and datasets.
results: MILO achieved superior performance on 3D scene understanding benchmarks, showing a +3.2% absolute gain in Acc@0.25 on ScanRefer and a +6.2% improvement in B-4@0.5 on Scan2Cap compared to baselines.

The framework improved spatial reasoning on VSI-Bench with an average accuracy of 61.7% (+2.2% over baseline), demonstrating significant gains in relative direction (+5.7%) and distance (+3.3%) reasoning tasks.

Ablation studies confirmed that visual generative tuning is crucial, with its removal leading to a 1.7% drop in Acc@0.25 on ScanRefer, and both RePE and GeoGen components significantly contributed to overall performance.

MILO effectively mitigated "visual illiteracy," achieving a significantly higher Grounded Attention Score (GAS) of 0.077 compared to 0.014 for models without visual generative tuning, indicating better attention grounding to relevant visual regions.
solution: Proposed MILO (implicit spatIaL wOrld modeling), a two-stage training paradigm that integrates visual generative tuning with a video diffusion model (Stage 1) before conventional verbal fine-tuning (Stage 2).
Introduced Relative Positional Encoding (RePE), a coordinate-agnostic method that models inter-frame camera-pose variations using geometric transformation matrices.
Created GeoGen, a large-scale, geometry-aware generative dataset comprising 2,241 videos and 267,827 "observation–action–outcome" triplets for novel view synthesis and trajectory generation tasks.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from MBZUAI, SYSU, and PKU developed MILO, an implicit spatial world modeling paradigm that enhances Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) by complementing verbal descriptive tuning with visual generative tuning. This method, incorporating a novel Relative Positional Encoding (RePE) and the GeoGen dataset, substantially improved 3D spatial reasoning, yielding a +3.2% absolute gain in Acc@0.25 on ScanRefer and increasing the Grounded Attention Score to 0.077.

title: Seeing through Imagination: Learning Scene Geometry via Implicit Spatial World Modeling

abstract: Recent advances in Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have showcased remarkable capabilities in vision-language understanding. However, enabling robust video spatial reasoning-the ability to comprehend object locations, orientations, and inter-object relationships in dynamic 3D scenes-remains a key unsolved challenge. Existing approaches primarily rely on spatially grounded supervised fine-tuning or reinforcement learning, yet we observe that such models often exhibit query-locked reasoning, focusing narrowly on objects explicitly mentioned in the prompt while ignoring critical contextual cues. To address this limitation, we propose Object-Centric 3D Rollout (OCR), a novel strategy that introduces structured perturbations to the 3D geometry of selected objects during training. By degrading object-specific visual cues and projecting the altered geometry into 2D space, OCR compels the model to reason holistically across the entire scene. We further design a rollout-based training pipeline that jointly leverages vanilla and region-noisy videos to optimize spatial reasoning trajectories. Experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art performance: our 3B-parameter model achieves 47.5% accuracy on VSI-Bench, outperforming several 7B baselines. Ablations confirm OCR's superiority over prior rollout strategies (e.g., T-GRPO, NoisyRollout).

keyInsights: Explicitly degrading object-specific visual cues through 3D spatial perturbations during reinforcement learning effectively overcomes query-locked reasoning in MLLMs by compelling them to rely on surrounding context.
A curriculum learning approach for object perturbation, starting with higher noise and gradually reducing it, fosters more robust and generalizable spatial reasoning paths.

Leveraging high-quality, CoT-annotated supervised fine-tuning data, specifically curated for challenging spatial problems, is critical for cold-starting and improving MLLMs before reinforcement learning.
originalProblem: Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) suffer from "query-locked reasoning," focusing narrowly on query-mentioned objects and neglecting crucial contextual cues for holistic spatial understanding.
Reinforcement Learning (RL) based methods for video spatial reasoning often overfit due to the scarcity of diverse, high-quality 3D indoor video data.
A fundamental lack of robust, human-like comprehension of object locations, orientations, and intricate inter-object relationships within dynamic 3D video scenes exists in current MLLMs.
results: The 3B-parameter model trained with OCR achieved an average accuracy of 47.5% on the VSI-Bench benchmark, surpassing larger open-source MLLMs like LLaVA-NeXT-Video-72B (40.9%) and proprietary systems such as GPT-4o (34.0%) and Gemini-1.5-Pro (45.4%).

Ablation studies confirmed that the curated OCR-SFT dataset, the Object-Centric 3D Rollout strategy, and a linear annealing schedule for object perturbation are crucial contributors to the observed performance gains.

Qualitative visualizations demonstrated that the model effectively incorporated unmentioned contextual objects into its reasoning and established a global spatial perspective, addressing the identified "query-locked reasoning" issue.
solution: Proposed Object-Centric 3D Rollout (OCR), an RL strategy that introduces structured spatial perturbations to selected objects' 3D geometry, forcing MLLMs to consider broader scene context.
Implemented a rollout-based training pipeline that leverages both original and region-noisy videos, where model parameters are updated solely from gradients computed from the original inputs.
Designed a step-wise annealed object selector that gradually decreases the number of perturbed objects and noise intensity, guiding the model from harder to easier reasoning scenarios.
Constructed high-quality OCR-SFT and OCR-RL datasets, incorporating comprehensive category coverage and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) annotations, with filtering for "hard" spatial reasoning problems.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers developed Object-Centric 3D Rollout (OCR), a novel reinforcement learning strategy to enhance Multi-modal Large Language Models' (MLLMs) video spatial reasoning by addressing "query-locked reasoning". A 3B-parameter model trained with OCR achieved 47.5% average accuracy on the VSI-Bench benchmark, outperforming larger MLLMs and proprietary models.

title: Video Spatial Reasoning with Object-Centric 3D Rollout

abstract: Large vision-language models (VLMs) show strong multimodal understanding but still struggle with 3D spatial reasoning, such as distance estimation, size comparison, and cross-view consistency. Existing 3D-aware methods either depend on auxiliary 3D information or enhance RGB-only VLMs with geometry encoders through shallow feature fusion. We propose SpaceMind, a multimodal large language model explicitly designed for spatial reasoning solely from RGB inputs. The model adopts a dual-encoder architecture, integrating VGGT as a spatial understanding encoder and InternViT as a 2D visual encoder. The key idea is to treat the camera representation as an active guiding modality rather than passive metadata. Specifically, SpaceMind introduces a lightweight Camera-Guided Modality Fusion module before the language model to replace shallow fusion. It applies camera-conditioned biasing to spatial tokens, assigns query-independent weights reflecting their geometric importance, and uses the camera embedding to gate the fused representation. Empirically, SpaceMind establishes new state-of-the-art results on VSI-Bench, SQA3D and SPBench, surpassing both open and proprietary systems on VSI-Bench and SPBench by large margins and achieving state-of-the-art performance on SQA3D. These results demonstrate that camera-guided modality fusion is an effective and practical inductive bias for equipping VLMs with genuinely spatially grounded intelligence. We will release code and model checkpoints to support future research.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen

keyInsights: Treating the camera representation as an active guiding modality, rather than passive metadata, is crucial for endowing VLMs with robust and coherent 3D spatial intelligence.

Explicitly disentangling and synergistically fusing viewpoint (camera), scene geometry (spatial tokens), and semantic content (visual tokens) provides a more principled and effective approach to spatial reasoning.

Viewpoint-dependent spatial biases and importance weighting for spatial tokens, combined with a camera-conditioned gating mechanism, significantly improve the integration and interpretation of spatial information.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language Models (VLMs) consistently struggle with explicit 3D spatial reasoning tasks such as metric distance estimation, object size comparison, and cross-view consistency.

Current 3D-aware MLLMs using RGB-only inputs often fuse spatial cues in an 'ad hoc' manner, treating camera information as passive metadata rather than an active guide, thus conflating viewpoint with scene content.

Methods relying on explicit 3D inputs (e.g., depth maps, point clouds) are limited by specialized hardware requirements and complex processing pipelines, making them impractical for many real-world applications.
results: SpaceMind achieves a new state of the art on VSI-Bench with an average accuracy of 69.6%, an 8.7-point improvement over the previous strongest baseline, showing particular gains (+30.5 points) in view-integration tasks like Appearance Order.
The model sets a new state of the art on the SQA3D benchmark across most question types using only video inputs, demonstrating strong 3D spatial cue recovery from RGB data.

SpaceMind substantially outperforms prior models on the out-of-domain SPBench dataset, including its single-image subset, indicating superior robustness and generalization capabilities in spatial reasoning.
solution: SpaceMind proposes a multimodal large language model architecture with a novel Camera-Guided Modality Fusion (CGMF) module that actively leverages camera representations to guide the fusion of spatial and visual information.

The CGMF module incorporates three mechanisms: Camera-Conditioned Spatial Bias (geoMLP) to dynamically adjust spatial features based on viewpoint; Per-Token Spatial Importance Weighting (twMLP) to scale spatial values based on reliability; and a Camera-Conditioned Gate (SwiGLU Fusion Network) to modulate spatially enriched features.

The architecture uses a dual-encoder setup (InternViT-300M for visual, VGGT for spatial and camera tokens) with an InternVL3-8B language model backbone, and trains trainable components with LoRA while keeping backbones frozen.
paper_summary.summary: SpaceMind, developed by Huawei and collaborators, introduces a Camera-Guided Modality Fusion (CGMF) approach that significantly enhances the 3D spatial reasoning capabilities of vision-language models from RGB-only inputs. The model achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks, including an 8.7-point improvement on VSI-Bench, by actively leveraging camera viewpoint information.

title: SpaceMind: Camera-Guided Modality Fusion for Spatial Reasoning in Vision-Language Models

abstract: Large vision-language models (VLMs) still struggle with reliable 3D spatial reasoning, a core capability for embodied and physical AI systems. This limitation arises from their inability to capture fine-grained 3D geometry and spatial relationships. While recent efforts have introduced multi-view geometry transformers into VLMs, they typically fuse only the deep-layer features from vision and geometry encoders, discarding rich hierarchical signals and creating a fundamental bottleneck for spatial understanding. To overcome this, we propose SpatialStack, a general hierarchical fusion framework that progressively aligns vision, geometry, and language representations across the model hierarchy. Moving beyond conventional late-stage vision-geometry fusion, SpatialStack stacks and synchronizes multi-level geometric features with the language backbone, enabling the model to capture both local geometric precision and global contextual semantics. Building upon this framework, we develop VLM-SpatialStack, a model that achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple 3D spatial reasoning benchmarks. Extensive experiments and ablations demonstrate that our multi-level fusion strategy consistently enhances 3D understanding and generalizes robustly across diverse spatial reasoning tasks, establishing SpatialStack as an effective and extensible design paradigm for vision-language-geometry integration in next-generation multimodal physical AI systems.

keyInsights: Shallow layers of geometry encoders retain sharp local structures and fine-grained geometric boundaries crucial for local spatial perception, while deeper layers capture more homogeneous global context relevant for high-level reasoning.

Simply fusing multiple layers of geometry features in a naive manner (e.g., adding to the visual pathway) leads to feature interference rather than synergy, necessitating a structured, progressive integration strategy.

A progressive hierarchical alignment strategy, where specific geometry encoder layers are mapped to corresponding LLM decoder layers, is optimal for preserving and leveraging spatial information effectively.
originalProblem: Large Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate limitations in robust 3D spatial reasoning, often prioritizing image-level semantic alignment over fine-grained geometry and spatial relationships.
Existing geometry-integrated VLMs commonly fuse geometric features only from the final layers of geometry transformers, discarding valuable hierarchical geometric cues from intermediate layers.

This single-level fusion approach creates a bottleneck, hindering comprehensive 3D spatial understanding and limiting performance on tasks requiring both low-level geometric perception and high-level relational reasoning.
results: VLM-SpatialStack achieved the best overall average score across VSI-Bench, SPAR-Bench, BLINK-Spatial, and CV-Bench, demonstrating superior cross-task transfer ability compared to naive fusion methods.
VLM-SpatialStack-5B established a new state-of-the-art on VSI-Bench among open-source models, outperforming geometry-aware MLLMs and exhibiting robust zero-shot generalization for route planning.

Both versions of SpatialStack (based on Qwen2.5 and Qwen3.5) outperformed baselines of similar scale on 2D and 3D subsets of CV-Bench, confirming the benefits of multi-level geometry feature stacking for unified spatial perception.
solution: SpatialStack introduces a layered geometry-language fusion approach that progressively integrates multi-level geometric features into the Large Language Model (LLM) decoder.
The framework extracts patch features from multiple distinct layers (e.g., shallow, middle, deep) of a frozen Visual Geometry Grounded Transformer (VGGT) geometry encoder.

These multi-level geometry features are spatially aligned, projected, and then progressively injected as additive residuals into corresponding layers of the LLM decoder, ensuring hierarchical fusion throughout the decoding process.
paper_summary.summary: A layered geometry-language fusion framework, SpatialStack, enhances 3D spatial reasoning in vision-language models by progressively integrating multi-level geometric features into the language backbone, achieving state-of-the-art performance across various spatial reasoning benchmarks including VSI-Bench and CV-Bench.

title: SpatialStack: Layered Geometry-Language Fusion for 3D VLM Spatial Reasoning

abstract: While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable success in 2D visual understanding, their ability to reason about 3D space remains limited. To address this gap, we introduce geometrically referenced 3D scene representations (GR3D). Given a set of input images, GR3D annotates objects in the images with unique IDs and encodes their 3D geometric attributes as textual references indexed by these IDs. This representation enables MLLMs to interpret 3D cues using their advanced language-based skills in mathematical reasoning, while concurrently analyzing 2D visual features in a tightly coupled way. We present a simple yet effective approach based on GR3D, which requires no additional training and is readily applicable to different MLLMs. Implemented in a zero-shot setting, our approach boosts GPT-5's performance on VSI-Bench by 8% overall and more than 11% on tasks that rely heavily on spatial layout understanding. Qualitative studies further demonstrate that GR3D empowers MLLMs to perform complex spatial reasoning with highly sparse input views.

keyInsights: Explicitly linking object-level 3D geometric information as textual attributes to annotated 2D images enables MLLMs to leverage their existing language-based mathematical reasoning and 2D visual understanding for enhanced 3D spatial cognition.

A zero-shot, training-free approach can achieve state-of-the-art 3D spatial reasoning performance by properly structuring input, enabling MLLMs to act as general-purpose spatial reasoners without additional model training.

Presenting geometric data in a language-accessible format fosters greater interpretability, allowing MLLMs to generate transparent, step-by-step chain-of-thought reasoning for complex spatial queries.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) exhibit limitations in 3D spatial reasoning, struggling with relationships like distance, direction, and object size, often performing below human benchmarks.
Integrating explicit 3D inputs (e.g., point clouds) into MLLMs typically requires fine-tuning, which is hindered by the scarcity of large-scale 3D scene-language datasets.
Existing zero-shot approaches for 3D integration face difficulties in effectively associating 3D priors with visual features or struggle with MLLM comprehension without fine-tuning.
results: GR3D, when integrated with GPT-5, achieved the highest overall score on the VSI-Bench, demonstrating an 8% improvement over standalone GPT-5 and establishing a new zero-shot state-of-the-art.

The method yielded substantial improvements on configurational tasks, including a 14% gain in object counting, 15% in relative distance, 20% in relative direction, and 11% in route planning (with GPT-5).

Qualitative studies indicated that GR3D enabled robust spatial reasoning even with sparse input views (e.g., four images per scene) and facilitated the inference of unannotated object locations based on geometric relationships.
solution: Introduces Geometrically Referenced 3D Scene Representations (GR3D), where objects in 2D images are annotated with unique IDs, and their 3D geometric attributes (e.g., bounding boxes, primitives) are translated into ID-indexed textual descriptions.
Implements an explicit text-image association by projecting object IDs onto 2D input images with depth-based occlusion checks, directly linking visual features to their textual geometric data.

Leverages MLLMs' inherent language understanding and mathematical reasoning capabilities to process the combined visual and textual geometric input in a zero-shot manner for precise 3D spatial queries.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers at Zillow Group developed Geometrically Referenced 3D Scene Representations (GR3D), a method that enhances Multimodal Large Language Models' (MLLMs) 3D spatial reasoning by integrating annotated 2D images with textual descriptions of 3D geometric attributes. This approach, which requires no fine-tuning, improved GPT-5's performance on the VSI-Bench by 8% and demonstrated robust generalization across tasks and sparse visual inputs.

title: Boosting MLLM Spatial Reasoning with Geometrically Referenced 3D Scene Representations

abstract: Unlocking spatial reasoning in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) is crucial for enabling intelligent interaction with 3D environments. While prior efforts often rely on explicit 3D inputs or specialized model architectures, we ask: can MLLMs reason about 3D space using only structured 2D representations derived from perception? We introduce Struct2D, a perception-guided prompting framework that combines bird's-eye-view (BEV) images with object marks and object-centric metadata, optionally incorporating egocentric keyframes when needed. Using Struct2D, we conduct an in-depth zero-shot analysis of closed-source MLLMs (e.g., GPT-o3) and find that they exhibit surprisingly strong spatial reasoning abilities when provided with structured 2D inputs, effectively handling tasks such as relative direction estimation and route planning. Building on these insights, we construct Struct2D-Set, a large-scale instruction tuning dataset with 200K fine-grained QA pairs across eight spatial reasoning categories, generated automatically from 3D indoor scenes. We fine-tune an open-source MLLM (Qwen2.5VL) on Struct2D-Set, achieving competitive performance on multiple benchmarks, including 3D question answering, dense captioning, and object grounding. Our approach demonstrates that structured 2D inputs can effectively bridge perception and language reasoning in MLLMs-without requiring explicit 3D representations as input. We will release both our code and dataset to support future research.

keyInsights: MLLMs can achieve strong 3D spatial reasoning capabilities by leveraging carefully constructed structured 2D inputs, even without direct 3D features as input.

Object-centric metadata and filtered object marks are crucial for effectively grounding spatial context and reducing ambiguity, while guide prompts and rotation-aligned BEV images are essential for complex reasoning tasks like relative direction.
Large-scale instruction tuning with step-by-step reasoning traces (using augmented QA pairs and `<think>` tokens) substantially enhances an MLLM's ability to perform complex spatial reasoning.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) traditionally struggle with robust 3D spatial reasoning and typically require explicit 3D representations like point clouds as direct inputs.
Existing 3D-input methods for MLLMs face limitations in adaptability, generalization across diverse textual queries, and architectural complexity.
Prior 2D-based approaches for inferring 3D spatial cues often omit crucial object appearance details and explicit spatial priors (e.g., coordinates, categories), limiting their effectiveness.
results: In zero-shot analysis, GPT-o3 achieved high scores with Struct2D inputs on VSI-Bench (e.g., 96.5% on relative distance and 94.4% on relative direction with ground-truth object annotations).

The Qwen2.5VL model fine-tuned on Struct2D-Set attained the highest average score (43.6%) among open-source MLLMs on VSI-Bench, with notable gains in relative direction (45.9%) and route planning (35.8%).

The instruction-tuned model demonstrated competitive performance on standard 3D question answering and visual grounding benchmarks (ScanQA, SQA3D, Scan2Cap, ScanRefer, Multi3DRefer), often outperforming methods reliant on explicit 3D point cloud inputs.
solution: Struct2D proposes a perception-guided prompting strategy that transforms raw egocentric RGB-D video inputs into structured 2D representations, including Bird's-Eye-View (BEV) images with object marks, object-centric metadata (category, 3D coordinates), and intelligently selected egocentric keyframes.

A large-scale instruction tuning dataset, Struct2D-Set, is automatically generated from over 6K indoor scenes, designed for open-source MLLMs to acquire robust 3D spatial reasoning skills using only 2D projected inputs during training.
The framework is applied in a zero-shot setting to closed-source MLLMs (e.g., GPT-o3) and also used to fine-tune an open-source MLLM (Qwen2.5VL) on the Struct2D-Set.
paper_summary.summary: Struct2D introduces a perception-guided framework enabling Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) to perform 3D spatial reasoning using structured 2D representations, bypassing direct 3D input. The framework, along with its automatically generated instruction tuning dataset Struct2D-Set, achieves an average score of 43.6% on VSI-Bench for open-source MLLMs and competitive performance on other 3D scene understanding benchmarks.

title: Struct2D: A Perception-Guided Framework for Spatial Reasoning in MLLMs

abstract: Recent advances in Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have significantly improved 2D visual understanding, prompting interest in their application to complex 3D reasoning tasks. However, it remains unclear whether these models can effectively capture the detailed spatial information required for robust real-world performance, especially cross-view consistency, a key requirement for accurate 3D reasoning. Considering this issue, we introduce Viewpoint Learning, a task designed to evaluate and improve the spatial reasoning capabilities of MLLMs. We present the Viewpoint-100K dataset, consisting of 100K object-centric image pairs with diverse viewpoints and corresponding question-answer pairs. Our approach employs a two-stage fine-tuning strategy: first, foundational knowledge is injected to the baseline MLLM via Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) on Viewpoint-100K, resulting in significant improvements across multiple tasks; second, generalization is enhanced through Reinforcement Learning using the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) algorithm on a broader set of questions. Additionally, we introduce a hybrid cold-start initialization method designed to simultaneously learn viewpoint representations and maintain coherent reasoning thinking. Experimental results show that our approach significantly activates the spatial reasoning ability of MLLM, improving performance on both in-domain and out-of-domain reasoning tasks. Our findings highlight the value of developing foundational spatial skills in MLLMs, supporting future progress in robotics, autonomous systems, and 3D scene understanding.

keyInsights: MLLMs, despite primary training on 2D data, possess significant latent potential for 3D spatial perception that can be activated through targeted training.
Explicitly training MLLMs on basic spatial skills like viewpoint understanding effectively "activates" and substantially enhances their broader spatial reasoning capabilities across diverse tasks.

A hybrid cold-start initialization during SFT, combining new dataset training with human-assisted pseudo Chain-of-Thoughts, is crucial for preserving instruction-following and coherent reasoning while injecting foundational knowledge.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with understanding fine-grained 3D spatial information and cross-view consistency, often defaulting to superficial 2D continuity.
Existing MLLMs lack foundational spatial skills, such as precise viewpoint understanding and spatial transformations, leading to unreliable performance in real-world 3D scenarios.
The inherent "visual-spatial intelligence" of MLLMs is underexploited due to a lack of targeted training strategies for 3D perception from 2D inputs.
results: Actial-7B achieved 92.2% accuracy on the Viewpoint-100K dataset, a substantial improvement from the baseline's 12.9%.
On the CV-Bench, Actial-7B scored an average of 83.5%, surpassing leading models like GPT-4o (79.4%) and Gemini-1.5-Pro (77.7%).

The model demonstrated near-mastery of foundational viewpoints with 99.2% accuracy on BLINK's multi-view component and showed comprehensive improvements across many subtasks on the challenging MMSI-Bench.
solution: Actial introduces a "Viewpoint Learning" task that simplifies 3D consistency evaluation into multiple-choice questions about relative horizontal translation and rotation between object-centric image pairs.
A new Viewpoint-100K dataset, comprising 100,000 object-centric image pairs with automatically generated question-answer pairs for relative viewpoints, is created to support this task.

A two-stage fine-tuning strategy is employed: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with hybrid cold-start initialization for foundational knowledge injection, followed by Reinforcement Learning using Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) for generalization enhancement.
paper_summary.summary: Actial, a framework developed by researchers including those from Nanjing University and The Chinese University of Hong Kong, activates latent 3D spatial reasoning in Multimodal Large Language Models by explicitly training them on foundational viewpoint understanding. It achieves 92.2% accuracy on its new Viewpoint-100K dataset and secures an 83.5% average score on CV-Bench, outperforming leading proprietary models such as GPT-4o and Gemini-1.5-Pro.

title: Actial: Activate Spatial Reasoning Ability of Multimodal Large Language Models

abstract: Multi-modal large language models (MLLMs) have shown remarkable progress in integrating visual and linguistic understanding. Recent efforts have extended these capabilities to 3D understanding through encoder-based architectures that rely on pre-trained 3D encoders to extract geometric features. However, such approaches suffer from semantic misalignment between geometric and linguistic spaces, resolution sensitivity, and substantial computational overhead. In this work, we present SAGE, the first end-to-end 3D MLLM that directly processes raw point clouds without relying on a pre-trained 3D encoder. Our approach introduces a lightweight 3D tokenizer that combines geometric sampling and neighbourhood aggregation with vector quantization to convert point clouds into discrete tokens--treating 3D data as a foreign language that naturally extends the LLM's vocabulary. Furthermore, to enhance the model's reasoning capability on complex 3D tasks, we propose a preference optimization training strategy with a semantic alignment-based reward, specifically designed for open-ended 3D question answering where responses are descriptive. Extensive experiments across diverse 3D understanding benchmarks demonstrate that our end-to-end approach outperforms existing encoder-based methods while offering significant advantages in computational efficiency, generalization across LLM backbones, and robustness to input resolution variations. Code is available at: this http URL.
title: Point Cloud as a Foreign Language for Multi-modal Large Language Model

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) that directly process RGB inputs for tasks like 3D localization and navigation have shown remarkable potential. However, we argue that these RGB-only approaches are fundamentally flawed in their ability to generalize across cameras. By ignoring camera parameters, they entangle an object's physical properties with the camera's perspective, creating an irresolvable ambiguity. We show this leads MLLMs to overfit to the training camera distribution, rather than learning true and generalizable 3D geometric principles. To address this, we propose Camera-Aware MLLM framework for spatial MLLMs. It learns generalizable spatial reasoning by: (i) injecting camera intrinsics via a dense embedding that conditions each visual token; (ii) introducing a camera-aware data augmentation strategy that synthetically varies camera parameters, forcing the model to disentangle camera properties from scene content; and (iii) distilling geometric priors from a 3D vision foundation model. Extensive experiments demonstrate that camera-aware MLLMs substantially outperform their naive counterparts, particularly in cross-camera generalization tests on spatially-grounded tasks, indicating that camera-awareness is not only beneficial but also a prerequisite for robust and generalizable spatial intelligence in MLLMs.

keyInsights: Explicitly integrating camera intrinsic parameters into MLLMs is essential for resolving geometric ambiguities and achieving robust spatial reasoning that generalizes across diverse camera setups.

Synthetically augmenting training data with varied camera intrinsics, alongside architectural mechanisms like Camera Ray Embedding, effectively disentangles scene content from camera geometry within MLLMs.

Distilling 3D geometric priors from specialized foundation models provides a strong inductive bias, grounding MLLMs in fundamental 3D principles, even when explicit intrinsic annotations are unavailable.
originalProblem: Current "RGB-only" MLLMs suffer from inherent geometric ambiguity in spatial reasoning because they disregard camera intrinsic parameters.
This omission creates "focal-depth" and "size-depth" ambiguities, forcing MLLMs to overfit to the specific camera geometries of their training data.
Such models exhibit significant performance degradation when applied to scenes captured by cameras with different intrinsic parameters, limiting their generalization capabilities.
results: The Camera-Aware MLLM demonstrated substantial robustness and consistent accuracy on 3D localization tasks, maintaining performance even when camera intrinsics were significantly altered, outperforming camera-agnostic baselines.
The framework achieved state-of-the-art performance on precise spatial reasoning benchmarks like SPAR-Bench, and also excelled on general benchmarks such as VSI-Bench, CV-Bench-3D, and BLINK-Spatial.

Ablation studies confirmed that the synergistic combination of Camera Ray Embedding, Camera-Aware Geometric Augmentation, and Geometric Prior Distillation led to significant improvements in cross-camera generalization, boosting the F1 score from 39.1 to 52.1 on a "zoomed-in" test set.
solution: The Camera-Aware MLLM (CA-MLLM) framework integrates camera parameters and geometric priors directly into visual tokens, avoiding input canonicalization.
It employs a Geometry-Aware Visual Encoder (GAVE) with Dense Camera Ray Embedding to enrich visual tokens with 3D line-of-sight information derived from camera intrinsics.

The framework utilizes Camera-Aware Geometric Augmentation to synthetically perturb camera intrinsics during training and incorporates Geometric Prior Distillation from pre-trained 3D vision foundation models.
paper_summary.summary: Current Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) struggle with generalizing spatial reasoning across different camera systems due to inherent geometric ambiguity from overlooking camera intrinsics. Researchers from DAMO Academy, Alibaba Group, and Nanyang Technological University developed a Camera-Aware MLLM framework that explicitly conditions visual tokens on camera parameters and geometric priors, leading to more robust spatial understanding and improved cross-camera generalization.

title: On the Generalization Capacities of MLLMs for Spatial Intelligence

abstract: Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in general-purpose perception and reasoning, but they still struggle with tasks that require spatial understanding of the 3D world. To address this, we introduce pySpatial, a visual programming framework that equips MLLMs with the ability to interface with spatial tools via Python code generation. Given an image sequence and a natural-language query, the model composes function calls to spatial tools including 3D reconstruction, camera-pose recovery, novel-view rendering, etc. These operations convert raw 2D inputs into an explorable 3D scene, enabling MLLMs to reason explicitly over structured spatial representations. Notably, pySpatial requires no gradient-based fine-tuning and operates in a fully zero-shot setting. Experimental evaluations on the challenging MindCube and Omni3D-Bench benchmarks demonstrate that our framework pySpatial consistently surpasses strong MLLM baselines; for instance, it outperforms GPT-4.1-mini by 12.94% on MindCube. Furthermore, we conduct real-world indoor navigation experiments where the robot can successfully traverse complex environments using route plans generated by pySpatial, highlighting the practical effectiveness of our approach.

keyInsights: Grounding MLLMs' spatial reasoning in explicit 3D geometry via reconstructive vision tools and visual programming yields superior performance compared to implicit or 2D cognitive map methods.
A modular API for 3D spatial operations allows MLLMs to generate interpretable, compositional programs for multi-step, multi-view spatial queries, enhancing transparency and verifiability.

The zero-shot visual programming paradigm effectively leverages advanced 3D computer vision techniques without gradient-based fine-tuning, demonstrating strong generalizability across various MLLMs and reconstruction backbones.
originalProblem: Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) consistently struggle with robust 3D spatial reasoning, especially in multi-view settings, limiting their application in robotics and embodied AI.
Current MLLM approaches often rely on implicit 'imagination' or 2D cognitive maps, which lack explicit geometric grounding and robustness for complex 3D tasks.
Sparse and costly explicit 3D supervision in MLLM training data hinders the development of reliable language-to-3D correspondences.
results: Achieved an overall accuracy of 58.56% on the MINDCUBE benchmark, outperforming the strongest proprietary model (GPT-4.1-mini) by 12.94% and specialized spatial models by 16.5%.

Successfully demonstrated zero-shot real-world robot navigation, guiding a quadrupedal robot through complex indoor environments with geometrically consistent motion plans, where MLLM baselines struggled.

Showed robustness to different MLLM code agents and 3D reconstruction backbones, with performance improvements consistently attributed to the 3D visual programming paradigm rather than the specific underlying MLLM.
solution: Developed `pySpatial`, a visual programming framework that enables MLLMs to generate executable Python programs, orchestrating a defined API of specialized spatial tools.
The API includes functions for 3D scene reconstruction from 2D image sequences, natural language descriptions of camera motion, novel view synthesis, and camera manipulation (e.g., rotate, move).

Implemented a multi-stage process where an MLLM generates a program, an interpreter executes it to produce explicit intermediate outputs (text or images), and the MLLM then integrates these outputs with the original query to form the final response.
paper_summary.summary: The pySpatial framework equips Multi-modal Large Language Models with explicit and robust 3D spatial reasoning by generating executable Python programs that orchestrate specialized 3D vision tools. This approach achieved 58.56% accuracy on the MINDCUBE benchmark, outperforming GPT-4.1-mini by 12.94%, and successfully guided real-world robot navigation.

title: pySpatial: Generating 3D Visual Programs for Zero-Shot Spatial Reasoning

abstract: Precise spatial understanding from multi-view images remains a fundamental challenge for Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), as their visual representations are predominantly semantic and lack explicit geometric grounding. While existing approaches augment visual tokens with geometric cues from visual geometry models, their MLLM is still required to implicitly infer the underlying 3D structure of the scene from these augmented tokens, limiting its spatial reasoning capability. To address this issue, we introduce Cog3DMap, a framework that recurrently constructs an explicit 3D memory from multi-view images, where each token is grounded in 3D space and possesses both semantic and geometric information. By feeding these tokens into the MLLM, our framework enables direct reasoning over a spatially structured 3D map, achieving state-of-the-art performance on various spatial reasoning benchmarks. Code will be made publicly available.

keyInsights: An explicit, compact, and non-redundant 3D cognitive map, inspired by human cognition, enables MLLMs to perform more direct and interpretable spatial reasoning.
Geometric features from 3D reconstruction models can sufficiently encode spatial information, making additional explicit 3D positional embeddings redundant when integrated effectively.

Spatially aggregating and updating visual tokens in a 3D map significantly reduces visual token redundancy, improving computational efficiency, particularly for long sequences, without performance degradation.
originalProblem: Current MLLMs lack explicit geometric grounding, making precise spatial understanding from multi-view images challenging.
Existing spatial augmentation methods often introduce redundant visual tokens, increasing computational load for MLLMs.
The high cost and complexity of acquiring and annotating 3D data limit the scale and diversity of training datasets for MLLMs.
results: Cog3DMap achieved state-of-the-art results, improving average scores by 8.7 percentage points on VSTI-Bench and 3.9 percentage points on VSI-Bench.
The framework demonstrated substantial token efficiency, reducing visual token count by up to 90.2% on long-horizon tasks (RoboFAC) while maintaining or improving accuracy.

Control experiments on Scan2Cap showed that fusing semantic and geometric features (f_k + Prj(g_k)) boosted C@0.5 from 65.2 to 79.4 (+14.2) over semantic features alone, confirming the efficacy of geometric grounding.
solution: A recurrent framework progressively builds a 3D Cognitive Map by integrating multi-view visual observations into a unified 3D memory, explicitly storing spatial coordinates with semantic and geometric features.
Semantic features are extracted from the MLLM's pre-trained vision encoder, while geometric features are derived from a pre-trained recurrent transformer (Point3R).
A memory update mechanism aggregates new visual information with existing memory by partitioning and updating tokens based on spatial overlap, ensuring a compact and non-redundant representation.
paper_summary.summary: Cog3DMap introduces a framework that constructs a compact and non-redundant 3D cognitive map from multi-view images to enhance Multimodal Large Language Models' (MLLMs) spatial understanding. This approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on spatial reasoning benchmarks, improving VSTI-Bench by 8.7 percentage points and VSI-Bench by 3.9 percentage points, while reducing visual token count by up to 90.2% on long-horizon tasks.

title: Cog3DMap: Multi-View Vision-Language Reasoning with 3D Cognitive Maps

abstract: Recent Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have shown high potential for spatial reasoning within 3D scenes. However, they typically rely on computationally expensive 3D representations like point clouds or reconstructed Bird's-Eye View (BEV) maps, or lack physical grounding to resolve ambiguities in scale and size. This paper significantly enhances MLLMs with egomotion modality data, captured by Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) concurrently with the video. In particular, we propose a novel framework, called Motion-MLLM, introducing two key components: (1) a cascaded motion-visual keyframe filtering module that leverages both IMU data and visual features to efficiently select a sparse yet representative set of keyframes, and (2) an asymmetric cross-modal fusion module where motion tokens serve as intermediaries that channel egomotion cues and cross-frame visual context into the visual representation. By grounding visual content in physical egomotion trajectories, Motion-MLLM can reason about absolute scale and spatial relationships across the scene. Our extensive evaluation shows that Motion-MLLM makes significant improvements in various tasks related to 3D scene understanding and spatial reasoning. Compared to state-of-the-art (SOTA) methods based on video frames and explicit 3D data, Motion-MLLM exhibits similar or even higher accuracy with significantly less overhead (i.e., 1.40$\times$ and 1.63$\times$ higher cost-effectiveness, respectively).

keyInsights: Egomotion data, readily available from low-cost IMUs, serves as a lightweight and effective metric anchor to ground visual observations in real-world scale, overcoming the limitations of monocular 2D vision.

A cascaded filtering pipeline that leverages motion, geometric, and visual cues sequentially can efficiently select a sparse set of informative keyframes, significantly reducing computational overhead without sacrificing accuracy.

An asymmetric cross-attention design enables comprehensive integration of egomotion and visual modalities, allowing motion to provide metric scale and visual context to enrich motion features, ultimately enhancing spatial reasoning.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) exhibit limitations in performing accurate 3D spatial reasoning, despite their proficiency in 2D visual and textual understanding.
Explicit 3D data integration methods (e.g., point clouds, depth maps) for MLLMs are computationally intensive and often require specialized sensing hardware.
2D-only video approaches in MLLMs struggle to reliably infer absolute metric scale, leading to ambiguities in object distance and size.
results: Motion-MLLM achieved a +8.6 CIDEr score improvement on ScanQA and a +4.3 EM@1 improvement on SQA3D over the strongest 2D-input baselines, indicating enhanced spatial and positional understanding.
On VSI-Bench, the framework showed a +9.6 improvement in overall score compared to the best prior baseline, particularly on measurement-oriented tasks like distance and size estimation.

It demonstrated higher cost-effectiveness, achieving competitive accuracy with explicit 3D-input models (e.g., LLaVA-3D) while being approximately 1.4 times faster, and outperforming SOTA 2D/3D models in cost-effectiveness by up to 1.63x with egomotion-guided keyframe filtering.
solution: The Motion-MLLM framework integrates concurrent egomotion data from IMUs with video streams into an MLLM to provide physical grounding for 3D scene understanding.
A cascaded motion-visual keyframe filtering module efficiently selects informative frames by progressively applying motion, geometric change, and visual token novelty checks.

An asymmetric two-layer cross-modal feature fusion module uses a GRU-based encoder for IMU data and cross-attention to effectively integrate egomotion cues and inter-frame visual context into visual tokens.
paper_summary.summary: The Motion-MLLM framework integrates egomotion data from Inertial Measurement Units (IMUs) with video to enhance 3D spatial reasoning in Multimodal Large Language Models. This approach improves scene understanding and grounding on various benchmarks, demonstrating increased accuracy and computational efficiency compared to methods relying on explicit 3D inputs.

title: Feeling the Space: Egomotion-Aware Video Representation for Efficient and Accurate 3D Scene Understanding

abstract: Current Large Language Models have achieved Olympiad-level logic, yet Vision-Language Models paradoxically falter on elementary spatial tasks like block counting. This capability mismatch reveals a critical ``spatial intelligence gap,'' where models fail to construct coherent 3D mental representations from 2D observations. We uncover this gap via diagnostic analyses showing the bottleneck is a missing view-consistent spatial interface rather than insufficient visual features or weak reasoning. To bridge this, we introduce \textbf{3ViewSense}, a framework that grounds spatial reasoning in Orthographic Views. Drawing on engineering cognition, we propose a ``Simulate-and-Reason'' mechanism that decomposes complex scenes into canonical orthographic projections to resolve geometric ambiguities. By aligning egocentric perceptions with these allocentric references, our method facilitates explicit mental rotation and reconstruction. Empirical results on spatial reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms existing baselines, with consistent gains on occlusion-heavy counting and view-consistent spatial reasoning. The framework also improves the stability and consistency of spatial descriptions, offering a scalable path toward stronger spatial intelligence in multimodal systems.

keyInsights: The VLM spatial intelligence gap is primarily due to the absence of a stable, view-consistent intermediate representation that effectively bridges egocentric visual perception with logical reasoning, rather than limitations in visual encoders or general reasoners.

Explicitly grounding spatial reasoning in canonical orthographic views provides a geometrically rigorous and computationally efficient mechanism for VLMs to resolve geometric ambiguities and simulate mental rotation and reconstruction.

A view-grounded warm-start from supervised fine-tuning is crucial for the stability and effectiveness of subsequent reinforcement learning, enabling it to consistently reinforce correct spatial behavior and reduce training variance.
originalProblem: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) demonstrate a "spatial intelligence gap," excelling in abstract logic but failing at fundamental spatial tasks involving 3D understanding from 2D views, such as counting occluded blocks.
VLMs struggle to form coherent 3D mental representations from ambiguous 2D inputs, leading to geometric uncertainty, errors, and spatial hallucinations.
Existing spatial reasoning benchmarks often lack the granularity required to effectively evaluate models' abilities in 2D-3D alignment, mental rotation, and projection.
results: 3ViewSense-4B-rl models achieved high accuracy on OrthoMind-3D in-domain tasks, reaching 95.0% (strict RL) and 94.4% (slack RL) on Block Count, a substantial improvement over baseline models which often scored below 20%.

The framework demonstrated robust generalization on out-of-domain data, with RL-refined models showing significant relative improvements on OOD Block Count (82.5%) and Object Position (63.0%), and positive transfer to public benchmarks like SPBench-SI (54.2% vs. 27.1% for base Qwen3-VL-4B-Instruct).

Qualitative analysis indicated that 3ViewSense consistently generates concise and structured spatial descriptions, effectively reducing the verbose and hallucinated reasoning patterns observed in baseline models.
solution: 3ViewSense introduces a "Simulate-and-Reason" mechanism that reformulates spatial reasoning as a two-stage probabilistic process: Mental Simulation to infer orthographic views, followed by View-Grounded Reasoning to solve the task.

The framework employs a two-stage training approach: Orthographic Mental Simulation (OMS) for learning to infer canonical orthographic views, and View-Grounded Reasoning (VGR) which is refined using GRPO-based reinforcement learning.

A diagnostic dataset, OrthoMind-3D, was curated with programmatically synthesized in-domain and stochastically generated out-of-domain data for block counting and object reasoning to train and evaluate mental perspective reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University and Chongqing University developed 3ViewSense, a "Simulate-and-Reason" framework that enhances Vision-Language Models' spatial intelligence by grounding reasoning in mentally induced orthographic views. This method achieves up to 95.0% accuracy on in-domain block counting tasks and demonstrates strong generalization, improving performance on out-of-domain and public spatial reasoning benchmarks.

title: 3ViewSense: Spatial and Mental Perspective Reasoning from Orthographic Views in Vision-Language Models

abstract: Advancing towards artificial superintelligence requires rich and intelligent perceptual capabilities. A critical frontier in this pursuit is overcoming the limited spatial understanding of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), where geometry information is essential. Existing methods often address this by rigidly injecting geometric signals into every input, while ignoring their necessity and adding computation overhead. Contrary to this paradigm, our framework endows the model with an awareness of perceptual insufficiency, empowering it to autonomously engage geometric features in reasoning when 2D cues are deemed insufficient. To achieve this, we first introduce an independent geometry input channel to the model architecture and conduct alignment training, enabling the effective utilization of geometric features. Subsequently, to endow the model with perceptual awareness, we curate a dedicated spatial-aware supervised fine-tuning dataset. This serves to activate the model's latent internal cues, empowering it to autonomously determine the necessity of geometric information. Experiments across multiple spatial reasoning benchmarks validate this approach, demonstrating significant spatial gains without compromising 2D visual reasoning capabilities, offering a path toward more robust, efficient and self-aware multi-modal intelligence.

keyInsights: Rigidly injecting 3D geometric features can be detrimental, introducing irrelevant noise and computational overhead for tasks that do not require explicit spatial understanding.
Endowing MLLMs with the internal ability to perceive the necessity of geometric information allows for adaptive, efficient, and robust reasoning across diverse tasks.

Training models with a curated dataset that highlights the benefits and drawbacks of geometric feature usage in specific contexts enables the learning of empirical priors for dynamic decision-making rather than fixed rules.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) lack explicit geometric representations, which limits their spatial reasoning abilities in real-world applications.

Current methods for incorporating 3D geometric information rigidly inject these features, often introducing noise, increasing computational overhead, and degrading performance on tasks that do not require spatial understanding.
MLLMs lack an internal mechanism to recognize when their standard 2D inputs are insufficient for a given task, preventing them from adaptively requesting necessary 3D cues.
results: GeoSense achieved the highest overall rank across 10 diverse benchmarks, with an average score of 56.6 on spatial reasoning and 55.9 on general visual benchmarks, demonstrating significant improvements over baselines.

The framework enhanced spatial reasoning performance (e.g., on BLINK benchmark) while successfully maintaining robust general visual reasoning, dynamically suppressing geometric features for non-spatial tasks.

Ablation studies confirmed that the adaptive selection scheme outperformed rigid geometric fusion, activating VGGT features for approximately 35.68% of samples on average and demonstrating the critical role of 'Perception Tuning' in balancing spatial gains with general understanding.
solution: GeoSense introduces a decoupled input architecture that treats 3D geometric information as an independent, on-demand modality, preventing contamination of 2D visual streams.

A two-stage supervised fine-tuning strategy, including 'Perception Tuning,' trains the MLLM to make 'Internal Sense Decisions' by triggering a specific token (<vggt>) when geometric features are deemed necessary.

A model-adaptive dataset is curated by analyzing performance discrepancies (with and without 3D geometry) for each sample, generating training signals that teach the model to activate or suppress geometric integration based on context.
paper_summary.summary: GeoSense equips multimodal large language models with the ability to autonomously determine when to utilize 3D geometric information, enhancing spatial reasoning capabilities while preserving general visual understanding. This adaptive integration strategy yielded superior performance across a diverse suite of benchmarks, outperforming baselines and models with rigid geometric integration.

title: GeoSense: Internalizing Geometric Necessity Perception for Multimodal Reasoning

abstract: Multi-modal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have advanced greatly in general tasks. However, they still face challenges in geometric reasoning, a task that requires synergistic integration of visual recognition proficiency and complex reasoning strength. Existing MLLMs prioritize optimizing the LLM backbone to enhance problem-solving capabilities, while rarely emphasizing improvements in discerning visual elements. However, we reveal that MLLMs suffer from severe visual perception deficiencies, including inaccurate geometric comprehension and severe visual hallucinations, which constrain their reasoning performance. To address this issue, we revisit geometric reasoning through a visual-centric lens that highlights the role of visual perception. To achieve this, we propose EAGLE, a novel coarse-to-fine visual enhancement framework that progressively leverages LLMs' guidance to improve perception proficiency. Specifically, given the substantial disparity between geometric diagrams and natural images, we first introduce Geometric Knowledge Injection. This process explores fundamental knowledge from diagram-caption data to enhance recognition capabilities and improve geometry-language alignments. Then, recognizing that different elements contribute unequally in the reasoning process, we introduce Geometric Knowledge Refinement. This stage leverages LLM-driven chain-of-thought solutions to guide the vision encoder in adaptively prioritizing key elements, fostering a synergistic interplay between visual comprehension and mathematical reasoning. Finally, we develop EAGLE, a geometry expert with strong perception and reasoning capabilities. Extensive experiments demonstrate its effectiveness on three popular benchmarks.
title: EAGLE: Elevating Geometric Reasoning through LLM-empowered Visual Instruction Tuning

abstract: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) have shown remarkable performance on diverse visual and linguistic tasks, yet they remain fundamentally limited in their understanding of 3D spatial structures. We propose Geometric Distillation, a lightweight, annotation-free fine-tuning framework that injects human-inspired geometric cues into pretrained VLMs without modifying their architecture. By distilling (1) sparse correspondences, (2) relative depth relations, and (3) dense cost volumes from off-the-shelf 3D foundation models (e.g., MASt3R, VGGT), our method shapes representations to be geometry-aware while remaining compatible with natural image-text inputs. Through extensive evaluations on 3D vision-language reasoning and 3D perception benchmarks, our method consistently outperforms prior approaches, achieving improved 3D spatial reasoning with significantly lower computational cost. Our work demonstrates a scalable and efficient path to bridge 2D-trained VLMs with 3D understanding, opening up wider use in spatially grounded multimodal tasks.

keyInsights: Transferring human-inspired geometric cues, such as sparse correspondences and relative depth, is an effective strategy to impart 3D understanding to 2D-trained VLMs.
Relative depth supervision is more robust and yields better generalizable geometric representations compared to using absolute depth values.
Powerful 3D foundation models can serve as effective "teachers" to distill rich, annotation-free geometric priors into 2D VLMs without explicit 3D reconstruction.
originalProblem: Existing Vision-Language Models (VLMs) trained on 2D image-text data inherently lack robust 3D spatial understanding and reasoning capabilities.
VLMs struggle with fundamental 3D tasks such as relative depth estimation, object occlusion, and precise spatial layout, limiting their real-world applicability.
Prior approaches to instill 3D awareness into VLMs are often computationally intensive, require explicit 3D annotations, or depend on generating large synthetic datasets.
results: Demonstrated superior performance on 3D visual understanding tasks, achieving significant gains in 3D correspondence (e.g., +11.87% PCK@0.05 on PF-PASCAL) and depth estimation (RMSE reduced from 0.432 to 0.367 on ScanNet++).
Enhanced 3D Vision-Language Model (VLM) understanding, improving exact match accuracy by +1.1% EM-1 on the ScanQA benchmark for 3D Visual Question Answering.
Achieved these results with substantially reduced computational cost, being approximately 54 times more efficient than prior state-of-the-art methods like FiT3D.
solution: Proposes Geometric Distillation, an annotation-free, architecture-agnostic fine-tuning framework for pretrained Vision-Language Models (VLMs).
Leverages parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA) to adapt a ViT-based CLIP model, minimizing changes to the original VLM architecture.

Distills robust geometric knowledge from powerful 3D foundation models by extracting three types of "human-inspired" geometric cues: sparse correspondences, relative depth information, and dense cost volume alignment.
paper_summary.summary: KAIST researchers developed Geometric Distillation, a lightweight and annotation-free framework that fine-tunes 2D-trained Vision-Language Models to acquire robust 3D spatial understanding by distilling geometric cues from 3D foundation models. The method achieves state-of-the-art performance on various 3D perception and 3D vision-language benchmarks while being significantly more computationally efficient than prior approaches.

title: 3D-Aware Vision-Language Models Fine-Tuning with Geometric Distillation

abstract: The rapid development of Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) has led to remarkable progress in 2D visual understanding; however, extending these capabilities to 3D scene understanding remains a significant challenge. Existing approaches predominantly rely on text-only supervision, which fails to provide the geometric constraints required for learning robust 3D spatial representations. In this paper, we introduce Reg3D, a novel Reconstructive Geometry Instruction Tuning framework that addresses this limitation by incorporating geometry-aware supervision directly into the training process. Our key insight is that effective 3D understanding necessitates reconstructing underlying geometric structures rather than merely describing them. Unlike existing methods that inject 3D information solely at the input level, Reg3D adopts a dual-supervision paradigm that leverages 3D geometric information both as input and as explicit learning targets. Specifically, we design complementary object-level and frame-level reconstruction tasks within a dual-encoder architecture, enforcing geometric consistency to encourage the development of spatial reasoning capabilities. Extensive experiments on ScanQA, Scan2Cap, ScanRefer, and SQA3D demonstrate that Reg3D delivers substantial performance improvements, establishing a new training paradigm for spatially aware multimodal models.
title: Reg3D: Reconstructive Geometry Instruction Tuning for 3D Scene Understanding

abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) demonstrate exceptional semantic reasoning but struggle with 3D spatial perception when restricted to pure RGB inputs. Despite leveraging implicit geometric priors from 3D reconstruction models, image-based methods still exhibit a notable performance gap compared to methods using explicit 3D data. We argue that this gap does not arise from insufficient geometric priors, but from a misalignment in the training paradigm: text-dominated fine-tuning fails to activate geometric representations within MLLMs. Existing approaches typically resort to naive feature concatenation and optimize directly for downstream tasks without geometry-specific supervision, leading to suboptimal structural utilization. To address this limitation, we propose GAP-MLLM, a Geometry-Aligned Pre-training paradigm that explicitly activates structural perception before downstream adaptation. Specifically, we introduce a visual-prompted joint task that compels the MLLMs to predict sparse pointmaps alongside semantic labels, thereby enforcing geometric awareness. Furthermore, we design a multi-level progressive fusion module with a token-level gating mechanism, enabling adaptive integration of geometric priors without suppressing semantic reasoning. Extensive experiments demonstrate that GAP-MLLM significantly enhances geometric feature fusion and consistently enhances performance across 3D visual grounding, 3D dense captioning, and 3D video object detection tasks.

keyInsights: The observed performance gap in 3D perception from RGB inputs is primarily due to a "geometry–semantics imbalance" in training, not a lack of geometric priors.
Explicit geometry-aligned pre-training with joint sparse 3D pointmap and semantic label prediction is crucial for activating and utilizing structural perception in MLLMs.

Geometric cues are distributed hierarchically across encoder layers, indicating that multi-level feature fusion, dynamically balanced by a token-level gating mechanism, is more effective than single-layer fusion.
originalProblem: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) using only RGB inputs exhibit limited 3D spatial perception, underperforming methods that utilize explicit 3D data.
Current MLLM training paradigms often suffer from a "geometry–semantics imbalance," where semantic supervision overshadows geometric representations.
This imbalance results in weakly activated and underutilized geometric features within MLLMs, leading to suboptimal structural perception and inaccurate spatial localization.
results: Improved 3D visual grounding on ScanRefer by over 11 Acc@0.25 percentage points compared to previous RGB-only methods, with GAP-MLLM-3B nearing explicit 3D-input model performance.
